questions.explanation: "So + adjective + that": so expensive that. No noun follows "so".

questions.question: She is ___ a good nurse that all the patients love her.
questions.explanation: "Such + a + adjective + noun": such a good nurse. Noun follows "such".

questions.question: The medication worked ___ quickly that she felt better the next morning.
questions.explanation: "So + adverb + that": so quickly that. Used with adverbs too, not just adjectives.

questions.question: It was ___ bad weather that all flights were cancelled. (so / such / too)
questions.explanation: "Such + (adjective +) noun": such bad weather. No article needed with uncountable nouns.

questions.question: He drove ___ fast that he got a speeding fine.
questions.explanation: "So + adverb + that": so fast that he got a fine.

questions.question: They were ___ kind neighbours that I was sad to move away.
questions.explanation: "Such + adjective + plural noun + that": such kind neighbours. No article with plurals.

questions.question: The hospital was ___ far from the bus stop that I had to take a taxi. (so / such / too)
questions.explanation: "So + adjective + that": so far that. No noun immediately follows "so".

questions.question: It was ___ useful information that I wrote it all down.
questions.explanation: "Such + (adjective) + uncountable noun": such useful information (no article).

questions.question: She spoke ___ clearly that everyone could understand her. (so / such / too)
questions.explanation: "So + adverb + that": so clearly that.

questions.question: It was ___ an interesting lecture that I stayed for two hours.
questions.explanation: "Such + a/an + adjective + singular countable noun + that".

questions.question: The instructions were ___ confusing that I had to call customer service. (so / such / too)
questions.explanation: "So + adjective + that": so confusing that. Adjective without a noun following.

questions.question: He was ___ a good doctor that patients came from other cities to see him.
questions.explanation: "Such + a + adjective + noun": such a good doctor.

questions.question: The immigration office had ___ long opening hours that I could go after work.
questions.explanation: "Such + adjective + plural noun": such long opening hours (no article).

questions.question: He was ___ nervous at the interview that he forgot his own name. (so / such / too)
questions.explanation: "So + adjective + that": so nervous that.

questions.question: It was ___ good news that she called all her friends immediately.
questions.explanation: "Such + adjective + uncountable noun": such good news (no article with uncountable).

questions.question: The queue at the DMV moved ___ slowly that I missed my lunch break.
questions.explanation: "So + adverb + that": so slowly that.

questions.question: They were ___ helpful staff that I left a five-star review. (so / such / too)
questions.explanation: "Such + adjective + plural noun": such helpful staff.

questions.question: The exam was ___ difficult that half the class failed.
questions.explanation: "So + adjective + that": so difficult that. Classic degree expression.

title: Question Tags — You're coming, aren't you?

questions.question: It's a beautiful day, ___?
questions.explanation: Positive statement → negative tag. "It is" → "isn't it?"

questions.question: She doesn't work here anymore, ___?
questions.explanation: Negative statement → positive tag. "Doesn't" → "does she?"

questions.question: You've been to the US before, ___? (have you / haven't you / didn't you)
questions.explanation: Positive statement with present perfect → negative tag: haven't you?

questions.question: They can't afford the rent, ___?
questions.explanation: Negative statement with "can't" → positive tag: "can they?"

questions.question: Your appointment is at 3pm, ___?
questions.explanation: Positive statement with "is" → negative tag: "isn't it?"

questions.question: He didn't sign the lease, ___? (did he / didn't he / does he)
questions.explanation: Negative statement with "didn't" → positive tag: "did he?"

questions.question: You'll call me if anything changes, ___?
questions.explanation: Positive statement with "will" → negative tag: "won't you?"

questions.question: The pharmacy closes at 9pm, ___?
questions.explanation: Positive statement with present simple → negative tag: "doesn't it?"

questions.question: We have to bring our passport, ___? (do we / don't we / have we)
questions.explanation: "Have to" in positive statement → negative tag: "don't we?" (not "haven't we").

questions.question: She was the one who called the ambulance, ___?
questions.explanation: Positive past statement with "was" → negative tag: "wasn't she?"

questions.question: Nobody told you about the change, ___?
questions.explanation: "Nobody" is negative → positive tag: "did they?" (they = everyone/people).

questions.question: Let's take a break, ___? (shall we / will we / do we)
questions.explanation: "Let's" always uses "shall we?" as the tag.

questions.question: You've already paid the deposit, ___?
questions.explanation: Positive present perfect → negative tag: "haven't you?"

questions.question: Nothing went wrong, ___?
questions.explanation: "Nothing" is negative → positive tag: "did it?"

questions.question: Pass me the form, ___? (will you / won't you / do you)
questions.explanation: Imperative sentences use "will you?" or "won't you?" as a tag.

questions.question: They moved into the new apartment last week, ___?
questions.explanation: Positive past simple → negative tag: "didn't they?"

questions.question: She won't have any problem getting the visa, ___? (will she / won't she / does she)
questions.explanation: Negative statement with "won't" → positive tag: "will she?"

questions.question: Everyone needs to register, ___?
questions.explanation: "Everyone" with positive statement → negative tag: "don't they?" (they = everyone).

questions.question: You hadn't heard the news, ___? (had you / hadn't you / did you)
questions.explanation: Negative past perfect → positive tag: "had you?"

questions.question: It must be difficult living so far from your family, ___?
questions.explanation: With "must" in positive sentences, the tag is often "isn't it?" (not "mustn't it").

title: Too & Enough — Too hot to drink, Old enough to drive

questions.question: The coffee is ___ hot to drink right now.
questions.explanation: "Too + adjective" = more than needed, causing a problem: too hot to drink.

questions.question: She is old ___ to apply for a driving licence.
questions.explanation: "Adjective + enough + to" = sufficient degree: old enough to apply.

questions.question: The apartment is ___ expensive for us to rent. (too / enough / very)
questions.explanation: "Too expensive" = more expensive than we can afford. A problem.

questions.question: He didn't get the job because he wasn't experienced ___.
questions.explanation: "Experienced enough" = the required level. "Enough" comes after adjectives.

questions.question: I was ___ nervous to speak at my job interview.
questions.explanation: "Too nervous to speak" = so nervous that speaking was impossible.

questions.question: There aren't ___ chairs for everyone. Can you bring more? (enough / too / very)
questions.explanation: "Enough + noun" = sufficient quantity: enough chairs. "Enough" comes before nouns.

questions.question: The medication is strong ___ to treat the infection.
questions.explanation: "Strong enough to treat" = has the required strength.

questions.question: She spoke ___ quietly for the people at the back to hear.
questions.explanation: "Too quietly for people to hear" = so quiet it caused a problem.

questions.question: He is ___ young to rent an apartment without a guarantor. (too / enough / so)
questions.explanation: "Too young" = younger than the minimum required age. A problem.

questions.question: Do we have ___ time to grab lunch before the appointment?
questions.explanation: "Enough time" = sufficient time. "Enough" before nouns.

questions.question: The queue was ___ long to wait in, so I booked online instead.
questions.explanation: "Too long to wait in" = more than acceptable. Caused me to choose differently.

questions.question: She didn't earn ___ money to pay for the visa fees. (enough / too / very)
questions.explanation: "Enough money" = the required amount. "Enough" before nouns.

questions.question: The patient wasn't well ___ to be discharged from hospital.
questions.explanation: "Well enough to be discharged" = reached the required health level.

questions.question: It's ___ cold to go outside without a jacket.
questions.explanation: "Too cold to go outside" = the cold is a problem that prevents going out.

questions.question: I didn't sleep ___ last night and now I can't concentrate. (enough / too / very)
questions.explanation: "Enough" after verbs: didn't sleep enough = not the required amount of sleep.

questions.question: She is ___ qualified for the position — she has a master's degree and five years experience.
questions.explanation: "More than enough qualified" — she exceeds the requirements.

questions.question: The contract was ___ complicated for me to understand without a lawyer. (so / too / enough)
questions.explanation: "Too complicated for me to understand" = exceeded my ability. A problem.

questions.question: There wasn't ___ parking near the hospital.
questions.explanation: "Enough parking" = sufficient spaces. "Enough" before nouns.

questions.question: He talked ___ fast for me to take notes properly.
questions.explanation: "Too fast for me to take notes" = speed caused a problem.

questions.question: She is confident ___ to give presentations in English now. (too / enough / very)
questions.explanation: "Confident enough to give presentations" = reached the required level of confidence.

title: Because, Since, As, So, Therefore — Reasons & Results

questions.question: I couldn't sleep ___ the neighbours were making too much noise.
questions.explanation: "Because" gives the direct reason: I couldn't sleep because of the noise.

questions.question: ___ she had an early appointment, she set three alarms.
questions.explanation: "Since" at the start of a sentence = because. Used when the reason is already known.

questions.question: He missed the last bus. ___, he had to walk home in the rain. (Therefore / Because / Since)
questions.explanation: "Therefore" = as a result / for that reason. Used formally to show a logical consequence.

questions.question: ___ the pharmacy was closed, I went to the emergency room instead.
questions.explanation: "As" at the start = because. It gives the reason in a slightly formal way.

questions.question: The rent went up, ___ many tenants had to find somewhere cheaper.
questions.explanation: "So" in the middle = result/consequence. The rent increase caused people to move.

questions.question: ___ you already have experience, you can skip the training. (As / So / Therefore)
questions.explanation: "As" at the start of a sentence gives a reason: as you already have experience.

questions.question: She couldn't take the medication ___ she was pregnant.
questions.explanation: "Because" is the most direct connector for giving a reason.

questions.question: The patient had a severe allergy. ___, the doctor chose a different medication.
questions.explanation: "Therefore" = as a result. Formal connector between two separate sentences.

questions.question: The office was closed on Monday ___ it was a public holiday. (because / so / therefore)
questions.explanation: "Because" gives the reason: closed because of the holiday.

questions.question: ___ the traffic was heavy, we left two hours earlier than planned.
questions.explanation: "Since" at the start = because. The heavy traffic was a known reason.

questions.question: He didn't have enough money for the deposit, ___ he couldn't move into the apartment.
questions.explanation: "So" shows the result of not having money.

questions.question: She studied hard every night. ___, she passed with distinction. (Therefore / Because / Since)
questions.explanation: "Therefore" formally introduces the logical result of the studying.

questions.question: ___ he had never driven on the right side of the road, he was nervous in the US.
questions.explanation: "As" at the start = because. It states the reason for his nervousness.

questions.question: The test results were negative. ___, no further treatment was needed.
questions.explanation: "Therefore" connects the test result to the logical conclusion.

questions.question: I didn't have the right documents, ___ I had to go back another day. (so / because / since)
questions.explanation: "So" shows the result: not having documents → had to go back.

questions.question: ___ you're new here, let me show you around the workplace.
questions.explanation: "Since" at the start = because. Gives the reason for offering to show around.

questions.question: The landlord refused to fix the heating. ___, we contacted the housing authority. (Therefore / Because / As)
questions.explanation: "Therefore" shows the logical consequence: refusal → contacted authorities.

questions.question: She lost her job ___ the company went bankrupt.
questions.explanation: "Because" gives the direct cause of losing the job.

questions.question: The highway was closed. ___, we had to take a longer route. (So / Because / Since)
questions.explanation: "So" shows the result of the highway being closed.

questions.question: ___ she is bilingual, she got the customer service job immediately.
questions.explanation: "As" at the start = because. Being bilingual was the reason she got the job.

title: Have Got — Possession & Description

questions.question: I ___ a terrible headache. I need some medicine.
questions.explanation: "Have got" = have. With I/you/we/they use "have got".

questions.question: She ___ an appointment at the clinic at 3pm.
questions.explanation: With he/she/it use "has got".

questions.question: ___ you got a pen I can borrow? (Have / Has / Do)
questions.explanation: "Have you got...?" is the question form with I/you/we/they.

questions.question: We ___ enough food in the fridge for the week.
questions.explanation: With "we" use "have got" for possession.

questions.question: He ___ a valid visa to enter the country. (have got / has got / is got)
questions.explanation: "Has got" with he/she/it for possession or necessity.

questions.question: I ___ any cash on me. Can I pay by card?
questions.explanation: Negative: "haven't got" = I don't have. With I/you/we/they.

questions.question: The pharmacy ___ the medication you need — you should try another one.
questions.explanation: Negative with he/she/it: "hasn't got".

questions.question: She ___ a driving licence yet. She is still learning. (haven't got / hasn't got / doesn't got)
questions.explanation: "Hasn't got" = doesn't have. With she/he/it.

questions.question: ___ your roommate got a spare key?
questions.explanation: Question with he/she/it: "Has + subject + got?"

questions.question: I ___ a doctor's appointment tomorrow morning.
questions.explanation: "Have got" with "I" for arrangements and possession.

questions.question: They ___ three children — two boys and a girl. (has got / have got / are got)
questions.explanation: "Have got" with "they" for family/possession.

questions.question: She ___ long dark hair and brown eyes.
questions.explanation: "Has got" for describing someone's appearance.

questions.question: I ___ a prescription from the doctor. I need to go to the pharmacy.
questions.explanation: "Have got" with "I" = I have a prescription.

questions.question: We ___ any more time to waste. The bus leaves in five minutes. (haven't got / hasn't got / don't got)
questions.explanation: "Haven't got" = we don't have. No more time available.

questions.question: Has she ___ enough experience for the job?
questions.explanation: "Has she got?" = Does she have? The past participle "got" comes after "has".

questions.question: My neighbour ___ a very friendly dog. It never barks.
questions.explanation: "Has got" with "my neighbour" (he/she = third person singular).

questions.question: I ___ a slight fever. I think I should rest today. (has got / have got / am got)
questions.explanation: "Have got" with "I" — used for describing symptoms or health.

questions.question: ___ they got enough blankets for the cold nights?
questions.explanation: Question with "they": "Have they got...?"

questions.question: The hospital ___ a new MRI machine. It was installed last week. (have got / has got / is got)
questions.explanation: "Has got" with "the hospital" (it = third person singular).

questions.question: I ___ a question about my lease agreement.
questions.explanation: "Have got a question" = I want to ask something.

title: This, That, These, Those — Near & Far

questions.question: ___ is my bus pass. I use it every day.
questions.explanation: "This" = singular, near. The bus pass is in my hand or close to me.

questions.question: ___ are the keys to your new apartment.
questions.explanation: "These" = plural, near. The keys are close to the speaker.

questions.question: Look at ___ building over there — it is the city hall. (this / that / these)
questions.explanation: "That" = singular, far away. The building is distant from the speaker.

questions.question: ___ people waiting over there are my colleagues.
questions.explanation: "Those" = plural, far away. The people are at a distance.

questions.question: Excuse me, ___ is my seat. You are sitting in the wrong place.
questions.explanation: "This" = singular, the seat near the speaker.

questions.question: ___ forms over there need to be filled in before your appointment. (This / That / Those)
questions.explanation: "Those" = plural, far away. The forms are at a distance.

questions.question: Can you pass me ___ bottle of water next to you?
questions.explanation: "That" = singular, away from the speaker (next to the other person).

questions.question: ___ is not a good neighbourhood to walk alone at night.
questions.explanation: "That" = singular, refers to a place away or a general situation.

questions.question: ___ medication right here is for your blood pressure. Take it twice a day. (This / That / These)
questions.explanation: "This" = singular, near. The medication is close to the speaker.

questions.question: Are ___ your children playing in the garden?
questions.explanation: "Those" = plural, away. The children are at a distance (in the garden).

questions.question: ___ is the pharmacy I told you about — the one on the corner. (This / That / These)
questions.explanation: "That" = singular, far away or being pointed to at a distance.

questions.question: ___ documents here are your visa application forms.
questions.explanation: "These" = plural, near. The documents are close to the speaker.

questions.question: Hello? ___ is Dr. Smith calling from City Hospital.
questions.explanation: "This is..." is used to introduce yourself on the phone.

questions.question: Who are ___ people standing outside our apartment building? (this / that / those)
questions.explanation: "Those" = plural, far. The people are at a distance outside.

questions.question: ___ was a really difficult interview. I was so nervous.
questions.explanation: "That was" refers to something just finished or remembered — past event.

questions.question: I need to return ___ library books by Friday.
questions.explanation: "These" = plural, near the speaker.

questions.question: ___ woman over there is the manager. You should speak to her. (This / That / Those)
questions.explanation: "That" = singular, far. The manager is pointed to at a distance.

questions.question: Can you sign ___ form right here for me, please?
questions.explanation: "This" = singular, near. The form is right here, close to both people.

questions.question: ___ were the best years of my life. (This / That / Those)
questions.explanation: "Those were" = plural, referring to past years now distant in time.

questions.question: ___ is a lot of money to pay for one month's rent.
questions.explanation: "That is a lot" = refers to an amount just mentioned or being discussed.

title: There is / There are — Describing Places

questions.question: ___ a supermarket near my apartment.
questions.explanation: "There is" + singular noun. One supermarket.

questions.question: ___ three bus stops on this street.
questions.explanation: "There are" + plural noun. Three bus stops.

questions.question: ___ a pharmacy on every corner in this city. (There is / There are / It is)
questions.explanation: "There is" + singular: a pharmacy (one pharmacy).

questions.question: ___ any milk left in the fridge?
questions.explanation: "Is there" + uncountable noun for questions: Is there any milk?

questions.question: ___ many job opportunities in this city if you speak English.
questions.explanation: "There are" + plural noun: many opportunities.

questions.question: ___ not a hospital in this neighbourhood — the nearest one is 20 minutes away. (There is / There are / It is)
questions.explanation: Negative singular: "There is not" (there isn't) a hospital.

questions.question: ___ any good English classes near here?
questions.explanation: "Are there" + plural noun for questions: any classes?

questions.question: ___ a problem with my lease agreement. Can you help?
questions.explanation: "There is a problem" — introduces the existence of a problem.

questions.question: ___ several clinics in this area. You have options. (There is / There are / There was)
questions.explanation: "There are" + plural: several clinics.

questions.question: ___ a waiting list for this apartment. You need to register.
questions.explanation: "There is a waiting list" — singular noun.

questions.question: ___ no parking spaces available right now. (There is / There are / There was)
questions.explanation: "There are no" + plural noun: parking spaces (plural).

questions.question: ___ a long queue at the immigration office this morning.
questions.explanation: "There is" + singular: a queue.

questions.question: ___ many things I need to do before I move out.
questions.explanation: "There are" + plural: many things.

questions.question: Excuse me, ___ a bank nearby? (is there / are there / there is)
questions.explanation: "Is there" + singular for questions: is there a bank?

questions.question: ___ any eggs in the fridge?
questions.explanation: "Are there" + plural for questions: any eggs?

questions.question: ___ a note from your doctor on the table.
questions.explanation: "There is" + singular: a note.

questions.question: ___ no direct bus from here to the airport. You need to change. (There is / There are / It is)
questions.explanation: "There is no" + singular: no direct bus.

questions.question: ___ a lot of noise in this building at night. I can't sleep.
questions.explanation: "There is" + uncountable noun: a lot of noise.

questions.question: ___ any good restaurants near the hospital? (Is there / Are there / There are)
questions.explanation: "Are there" + plural for questions: any restaurants?

questions.question: ___ a fee for late payment of rent.
questions.explanation: "There is a fee" — singular, introduces the existence of a fee.

title: Imperative — Instructions, Warnings & Requests

questions.question: The sign says: "___ your hands before handling food."
questions.explanation: Imperative = base verb with no subject. Used for instructions: Wash your hands.

questions.question: The doctor says: "___ your medication every morning with water."
questions.explanation: Imperative for instructions: Take your medication.

questions.question: ___ the door when you leave — it's cold outside. (Close / Closing / You close)
questions.explanation: Imperative = base form: Close the door. No subject needed.

questions.question: The sign says: "___ in this area — hospital zone."
questions.explanation: Negative imperative = "Don't + base verb": Don't smoke.

questions.question: The nurse says: "___ a deep breath and relax."
questions.explanation: Imperative for requests/instructions: Take a deep breath.

questions.question: ___ your seatbelt before the car moves. (Fasten / Fastening / You fasten)
questions.explanation: Imperative = base verb: Fasten your seatbelt.

questions.question: The sign says: "___ your rubbish in the bin."
questions.explanation: Imperative for public instructions: Put your rubbish in the bin.

questions.question: The teacher says: "___ your phones away during the exam."
questions.explanation: Imperative instruction: Put your phones away.

questions.question: ___ cross the road without looking both ways. (Don't / Not / No)
questions.explanation: Negative imperative: "Don't + base verb": Don't cross.

questions.question: The landlord says: "___ loud music after 10pm please."
questions.explanation: Negative imperative: Don't play loud music.

questions.question: ___ quiet in the library please. (Be / Being / You be)
questions.explanation: Imperative with "be": Be quiet. Use the base form of "be".

questions.question: The pharmacist says: "___ these tablets with food — not on an empty stomach."
questions.explanation: Imperative for medical instructions: Take these tablets with food.

questions.question: The sign says: "___ your shoes before entering."
questions.explanation: Imperative = base verb: Remove your shoes.

questions.question: ___ worry — the doctor will see you soon. (Don't / Not / No)
questions.explanation: Negative imperative: Don't worry — reassurance.

questions.question: The sign at the border says: "___ your passport ready."
questions.explanation: Imperative: Have your passport ready.

questions.question: The doctor says: "___ too much salt. It's bad for your blood pressure."
questions.explanation: Negative imperative: Don't eat too much salt.

questions.question: ___ me know if you have any questions. (Let / Letting / You let)
questions.explanation: "Let me know" is a common imperative expression meaning "tell me".

questions.question: The sign says: "___ the emergency exit blocked at all times."
questions.explanation: Negative imperative: Don't leave something blocked.

questions.question: ___ careful when you cross the road — traffic is very fast here. (Be / Being / Don't be)
questions.explanation: "Be careful" — imperative with "be" + adjective for warnings.

questions.question: The sign at the clinic says: "___ a seat and wait to be called."
questions.explanation: Imperative: Take a seat = please sit down.

title: Possessive 's — My sister's apartment, The doctor's office

questions.question: Where is ___ office? I have an appointment at 2pm.
questions.explanation: Possessive 's: the doctor's office = the office that belongs to the doctor.

questions.question: I borrowed ___ car to get to the hospital.
questions.explanation: Possessive 's: my neighbour's car = the car belonging to my neighbour.

questions.question: We are staying at ___ house this weekend. (my cousin's / my cousins / my cousin)
questions.explanation: Possessive 's for one cousin: my cousin's house.

questions.question: The ___ waiting room is on the second floor.
questions.explanation: Possessive 's for one dentist: the dentist's waiting room.

questions.question: Can I use ___ phone? My battery is dead.
questions.explanation: Possessive 's: your friend's phone.

questions.question: The ___ hours are 9am to 5pm Monday to Friday. (pharmacy's / pharmacys' / pharmacy)
questions.explanation: Possessive 's: the pharmacy's hours = the hours of the pharmacy.

questions.question: I live five minutes from ___ apartment.
questions.explanation: Possessive 's: my sister's apartment.

questions.question: What is ___ name? I need to fill in this form.
questions.explanation: Possessive 's: your mother's name.

questions.question: We ate at ___ restaurant last night. The food was amazing. (Sam's / Sams' / Sam)
questions.explanation: Possessive 's for a person's name: Sam's restaurant.

questions.question: The ___ salary is paid on the last day of every month.
questions.explanation: Possessive 's for one employee: the employee's salary.

questions.question: I found ___ wallet on the bus. I gave it to the driver. (someone's / someones' / someone)
questions.explanation: Possessive 's: someone's wallet = the wallet belonging to an unknown person.

questions.question: The ___ appointment is at 10am tomorrow.
questions.explanation: Possessive 's: the patient's appointment.

questions.question: Do you know ___ address? I need to send a letter.
questions.explanation: Possessive 's: your landlord's address.

questions.question: The ___ bedroom is the biggest room in the apartment. (children's / childrens' / children)
questions.explanation: "Children" is already plural (irregular), so we add 's: children's bedroom.

questions.question: I stayed at ___ house when I first arrived in the country.
questions.explanation: Possessive 's: my uncle's house.

questions.question: The ___ signature is required on this contract.
questions.explanation: Possessive 's: the manager's signature.

questions.question: What is ___ phone number? I want to call them. (the clinic's / the clinics' / the clinic)
questions.explanation: Possessive 's: the clinic's phone number.

questions.question: I read ___ report about the new medication.
questions.explanation: Possessive 's: the researcher's report.

questions.question: We celebrated ___ birthday at a nice restaurant. (my friend's / my friends' / my friend)
questions.explanation: One friend: my friend's birthday.

questions.question: The ___ room is at the end of the corridor.
questions.explanation: Possessive 's: the nurse's room.

title: Have to / Must / Mustn't — Obligation & Prohibition

questions.question: You ___ show your ID to enter the building. It's the rules.
questions.explanation: Both "must" and "have to" express obligation. "Must" is often personal/internal; "have to" is often external rules.

questions.question: I ___ take this medicine three times a day. The doctor told me.
questions.explanation: "Have to" for external obligation — the doctor gave this instruction.

questions.question: You ___ smoke in the hospital. It is strictly forbidden. (mustn't / don't have to / must)
questions.explanation: "Mustn't" = prohibition — it is not allowed. Very different from "don't have to".

questions.question: She ___ renew her visa before it expires, or she will have to leave.
questions.explanation: "Has to" for a necessary external obligation with consequences.

questions.question: You ___ bring food to the exam. It's optional but allowed.
questions.explanation: "Don't have to" = no obligation — it is not necessary but you can if you want.

questions.question: Employees ___ wear a uniform. It is company policy. (have to / don't have to / mustn't)
questions.explanation: "Have to" for rules and external obligations.

questions.question: You ___ tell anyone my password. It's private.
questions.explanation: "Mustn't" = strong prohibition — it would be wrong to tell anyone.

questions.question: We ___ book a table — the restaurant is always empty on weekdays.
questions.explanation: "Don't have to" = it's not necessary. You can walk in without booking.

questions.question: You ___ drink and drive. It is dangerous and illegal. (mustn't / don't have to / should)
questions.explanation: "Mustn't" = prohibition — it is both dangerous and against the law.

questions.question: I ___ call the landlord today — the heating has stopped working.
questions.explanation: "Must" for strong personal necessity — the situation requires action.

questions.question: Students ___ attend all classes. Attendance is mandatory. (have to / don't have to / mustn't)
questions.explanation: "Have to" for external rules — attendance is required by the institution.

questions.question: You ___ pay to enter the museum on Sundays — it's free.
questions.explanation: "Don't have to" = no obligation to pay — entry is free.

questions.question: Passengers ___ keep their seatbelts on during takeoff.
questions.explanation: "Must" for strong rules on planes — safety obligation.

questions.question: You ___ leave food in the shared fridge for more than three days. It's the house rule. (mustn't / don't have to / must)
questions.explanation: "Mustn't" = house rule prohibition.

questions.question: She ___ work tomorrow — it's her day off.
questions.explanation: "Doesn't have to" = no obligation. She is free to rest.

questions.question: You ___ see a specialist if the pain continues — it could be serious.
questions.explanation: "Must see" for strong personal advice/necessity.

questions.question: He ___ register for the course by Friday or he will lose his place. (has to / doesn't have to / mustn't)
questions.explanation: "Has to" for external deadline — necessary action with consequences.

questions.question: You ___ use your real name on official government forms.
questions.explanation: "Must use" for legal/official obligation.

questions.question: The children ___ play near the road. It's very dangerous. (mustn't / don't have to / must)
questions.explanation: "Mustn't play near the road" — strong prohibition for safety.

questions.question: I ___ go to the post office today. The package will be sent automatically online.
questions.explanation: "Don't have to go" = not necessary — the online option removes the obligation.

title: Countable & Uncountable Nouns — much, many, a few, a little

questions.question: How ___ water do you drink every day?
questions.explanation: "Much" is used with uncountable nouns. Water cannot be counted individually.

questions.question: How ___ people live in your apartment building?
questions.explanation: "Many" is used with countable nouns. People can be counted.

questions.question: I only have ___ money left this week. I need to be careful. (a little / a few / many)
questions.explanation: "A little" + uncountable noun. Money is uncountable.

questions.question: There are ___ good hospitals in this city. You have good options.
questions.explanation: "A few" + countable plural noun. Hospitals can be counted.

questions.question: She doesn't have ___ experience in customer service.
questions.explanation: "Much" + uncountable noun in negatives. Experience is uncountable.

questions.question: I need to buy ___ things from the supermarket — just milk and bread. (a few / a little / much)
questions.explanation: "A few" + countable plural: a few things (things can be counted).

questions.question: We don't have ___ time left. The pharmacy closes in ten minutes.
questions.explanation: "Much time" in negatives — time is uncountable.

questions.question: There was ___ traffic on the way to the hospital — only a few cars.
questions.explanation: "Much traffic" — traffic is uncountable. A little traffic / not much traffic.

questions.question: She has ___ friends in this city because she just moved here. (a few / a little / much)
questions.explanation: "A few friends" — friends are countable. A few = a small number (positive sense).

questions.question: Could I have ___ milk in my tea please?
questions.explanation: "A little" + uncountable noun: a little milk (a small amount).

questions.question: How ___ days did you have to wait for the visa? (much / many / few)
questions.explanation: "How many days" — days are countable.

questions.question: I only have ___ English, but I'm learning more every day.
questions.explanation: "A little English" — language ability is uncountable.

questions.question: There aren't ___ vegetarian options at this restaurant.
questions.explanation: "Many options" — options are countable. Negative: not many options.

questions.question: I need ___ advice about my visa application. Can you help? (a few / a little / many)
questions.explanation: "A little advice" — advice is uncountable (no plural: not "advices").

questions.question: She takes ___ medications every morning for different conditions.
questions.explanation: "A few" or "several" + countable plural: medications are countable.

questions.question: How ___ sugar do you want in your coffee?
questions.explanation: "How much sugar" — sugar is uncountable.

questions.question: There is ___ furniture in the apartment — just a bed and a table. (a little / a few / many)
questions.explanation: "A little furniture" — furniture is uncountable (no plural: not "furnitures").

questions.question: I have ___ questions about my contract. Can we talk?
questions.explanation: "A few questions" — questions are countable. A few = a small number.

questions.question: We don't have ___ information about the new rules yet. (many / much / a few)
questions.explanation: "Much information" — information is uncountable (no plural: not "informations").

questions.question: He only earns ___ money, so he shares an apartment with three others.
questions.explanation: "A little money" — money is uncountable. A little = a small amount.

title: Some & Any — Positive, Negative & Questions

questions.question: There is ___ milk in the fridge if you want some.
questions.explanation: "Some" in positive sentences. There is a quantity of milk available.

questions.question: Is there ___ post office near here?
questions.explanation: "Any" in questions with singular countable nouns: Is there any post office?

questions.question: I don't have ___ change for the bus. Do you have any? (some / any / a)
questions.explanation: "Any" in negative sentences: I don't have any change.

questions.question: Would you like ___ tea or coffee?
questions.explanation: "Some" in offers and requests: Would you like some...? (offering something)

questions.question: There aren't ___ available apartments in this building right now.
questions.explanation: "Any" in negative sentences with plural nouns: aren't any apartments.

questions.question: Can I have ___ water please? I'm very thirsty. (some / any / a)
questions.explanation: "Some" in requests: Can I have some water? (asking for something politely).

questions.question: Are there ___ side effects with this medication?
questions.explanation: "Any" in questions: Are there any side effects?

questions.question: I need ___ help filling in this form. It's complicated.
questions.explanation: "Some" in positive sentences with uncountable nouns: some help.

questions.question: She doesn't have ___ experience driving on the right side of the road. (some / any / a)
questions.explanation: "Any" in negative sentences with uncountable nouns: doesn't have any experience.

questions.question: There are ___ good English schools in this neighbourhood.
questions.explanation: "Some" in positive statements: some good schools (a number of them).

questions.question: Do you have ___ identification with you? (some / any / a)
questions.explanation: "Any" in questions with uncountable nouns: Do you have any identification?

questions.question: She bought ___ groceries on her way home from work.
questions.explanation: "Some" in positive sentences: she bought some groceries.

questions.question: There isn't ___ parking outside the hospital — you should use the car park.
questions.explanation: "Any" in negative sentences with uncountable nouns: isn't any parking.

questions.question: Would you like ___ help with your luggage? (some / any / a)
questions.explanation: "Some" in offers: Would you like some help? (offering assistance).

questions.question: I have ___ questions about the lease — is now a good time?
questions.explanation: "Some" in positive sentences: I have some questions (a number of them).

questions.question: Did you bring ___ food? I'm a little hungry.
questions.explanation: "Any" in questions: Did you bring any food?

questions.question: There is ___ sugar in the cupboard if you need it. (some / any / a)
questions.explanation: "Some" in positive sentences: there is some sugar (a quantity of it).

questions.question: I don't know ___ people in this city yet — I just arrived.
questions.explanation: "Any people" in negative sentences.

questions.question: Could you give me ___ information about the rental process? (some / any / a)
questions.explanation: "Some" in polite requests: Could you give me some information?

questions.question: Is there ___ chance of getting an appointment today?
questions.explanation: "Any chance" in questions — asking if a possibility exists.

title: Stative Verbs — know, understand, believe, love, hate

questions.question: I ___ what you mean. Please explain again.
questions.explanation: "Understand" is a stative verb — it describes a mental state, not an action. We use simple tense, not continuous.

questions.question: She ___ three languages — English, Burmese and Thai.
questions.explanation: "Know" is stative — use present simple, not present continuous.

questions.question: I ___ spicy food. I eat it every day. (am loving / love / am love)
questions.explanation: "Love" is a stative verb. We say "I love" not "I am loving" in most cases.

questions.question: He ___ that he is right, but I disagree.
questions.explanation: "Believe" is stative — describes an opinion/state of mind. Use simple tense.

questions.question: This soup ___ amazing. What's in it?
questions.explanation: "Taste" as a stative verb (describing quality) uses simple tense: tastes amazing.

questions.question: She ___ living alone — she prefers company. (hates / is hating / hate)
questions.explanation: "Hate" is stative — describes a feeling/preference. Use present simple.

questions.question: I ___ the answer to your question.
questions.explanation: "Know" is stative. Never say "I am knowing" — always "I know".

questions.question: This medication ___ very bitter. Are you sure it's the right one?
questions.explanation: "Taste" as a stative verb (perception): tastes bitter.

questions.question: I ___ my new apartment. It's perfect. (love / am loving / is love)
questions.explanation: "Love" is stative for emotions and states: I love my apartment.

questions.question: She ___ to the doctor — she is very scared of needles.
questions.explanation: "Want" is stative — use simple tense: doesn't want.

questions.question: He ___ a lot about computers — he can help you. (knows / is knowing / know)
questions.explanation: "Know" is stative. With he/she/it use "knows" (present simple).

questions.question: I ___ what you are saying but I disagree.
questions.explanation: "Understand" is stative — describes mental comprehension. Use simple tense.

questions.question: The apartment ___ very expensive for the size.
questions.explanation: "Seem" is stative — describes an appearance or impression. Use simple tense.

questions.question: I ___ my new colleagues at work — they are very friendly. (like / am liking / liking)
questions.explanation: "Like" is stative for preferences. "I like" not "I am liking".

questions.question: She ___ that her English is getting better every month.
questions.explanation: "Feel" as a stative verb for opinions/beliefs: she feels that... Use simple tense.

questions.question: This coffee ___ very strong. Did you add extra sugar?
questions.explanation: "Smell" as stative (describing quality) uses simple tense: smells strong.

questions.question: I ___ what to do in this situation. It's very confusing. (don't know / am not knowing / not know)
questions.explanation: "Know" is stative. Negative: "don't know" not "am not knowing".

questions.question: He ___ the person who called. He had never met them before.
questions.explanation: "Recognise" is stative — describes a mental process. Use simple tense.

questions.question: She ___ the idea of working night shifts. (hates / is hating / hate)
questions.explanation: "Hate" is stative. With she use "hates" (present simple).

questions.question: I ___ this is the right address — let me double check.
questions.explanation: "Think" as stative (opinion) uses simple tense: I think. ("I'm thinking" = actively considering something).

title: May & Might — Permission & Possibility

questions.question: ___ I use your phone to call the clinic? Mine is out of battery.
questions.explanation: "May I...?" is the polite way to ask for permission. More formal than "Can I?"

questions.question: She ___ be at home — I'm not sure. Try calling her.
questions.explanation: "May" for possibility when we are not certain — she is possibly at home.

questions.question: It ___ rain later. The sky looks a bit cloudy. (may / must / will)
questions.explanation: "May" or "might" for uncertain possibility about the future.

questions.question: ___ I sit here? Is this seat taken?
questions.explanation: "May I sit here?" — formal polite request for permission.

questions.question: He ___ not come to the appointment — he wasn't feeling well this morning.
questions.explanation: "Might not" for uncertain possibility — we are not sure if he will come.

questions.question: ___ I ask you a personal question? (May / Might / Should)
questions.explanation: "May I...?" is the polite formal form for requesting permission to do something.

questions.question: The results ___ take up to two weeks — it depends on the lab.
questions.explanation: "May take" for uncertain possibility — not guaranteed timing.

questions.question: You ___ park here between 6pm and 8am only.
questions.explanation: "May" for giving official permission: you are allowed to park here.

questions.question: She ___ need an operation — the doctor is still deciding. (may / must / will)
questions.explanation: "May need" — the doctor hasn't decided yet, so it's a possibility.

questions.question: I ___ be a little late — there's a lot of traffic right now.
questions.explanation: "Might be late" — uncertain, depends on traffic.

questions.question: ___ we leave a little earlier to avoid the rush hour? (May / Might / Should)
questions.explanation: "May we...?" is a formal/polite way to make a suggestion or request.

questions.question: The medicine ___ cause some drowsiness — be careful when driving.
questions.explanation: "Might cause" — possible side effect, not certain for everyone.

questions.question: Students ___ use a dictionary during the test — the teacher allows it.
questions.explanation: "May" for giving permission: you are allowed to use a dictionary.

questions.question: He ___ not understand everything yet — he only arrived last week. (may / must / will)
questions.explanation: "May not understand" — possible that he doesn't understand everything.

questions.question: I'm not sure about the appointment — I ___ have got the time wrong.
questions.explanation: "Might have got wrong" — uncertain past possibility about a mistake.

questions.question: ___ I take a photo in here? I want to remember this place.
questions.explanation: "May I take a photo?" — polite request for permission.

questions.question: There ___ be a delay on the subway today — there was an incident. (may / must / will)
questions.explanation: "May be a delay" — possible but not confirmed.

questions.question: Visitors ___ use the lift — the stairs are only for staff.
questions.explanation: "May use the lift" — visitors are given permission to use it.

questions.question: She ___ be interested in the job — she mentioned she was looking. (might / must / will)
questions.explanation: "Might be interested" — uncertain possibility based on something she said.

questions.question: You ___ leave early if you finish everything before 5pm.
questions.explanation: "May leave early" — permission is given, contingent on finishing work.

title: Do vs Make — do homework, make a mistake

questions.question: I need to ___ the laundry before I go to bed.
questions.explanation: "Do the laundry" — household tasks and chores use "do".

questions.question: She ___ a terrible mistake on her visa application.
questions.explanation: "Make a mistake" — creating or producing errors always uses "make".

questions.question: I need to ___ some research before my appointment. (do / make)
questions.explanation: "Do research" — activities and tasks use "do".

questions.question: He ___ a complaint to the landlord about the broken heating.
questions.explanation: "Make a complaint" — creating formal communication uses "make".

questions.question: Can you ___ me a favour and mail this letter?
questions.explanation: "Do someone a favour" — fixed expression with "do".

questions.question: She ___ a lot of progress in her English classes. (did / made)
questions.explanation: "Make progress" — achievement and creation use "make".

questions.question: I need to ___ the dishes after dinner.
questions.explanation: "Do the dishes" — household chores use "do".

questions.question: He ___ a good impression at his job interview.
questions.explanation: "Make an impression" — creating an effect uses "make".

questions.question: I ___ my best to understand the instructions. (did / made)
questions.explanation: "Do your best" — fixed expression with "do".

questions.question: She ___ an appointment with the specialist for next Thursday.
questions.explanation: "Make an appointment" — arranging/creating always uses "make".

questions.question: He ___ a lot of exercise when he was younger. (did / made)
questions.explanation: "Do exercise" — physical activities use "do".

questions.question: Can you ___ sure the door is locked before you leave?
questions.explanation: "Make sure" — fixed expression: ensure something is the case.

questions.question: I need to ___ some phone calls this afternoon.
questions.explanation: "Make phone calls" — creating communication uses "make".

questions.question: She always ___ her homework on the bus to school. (does / makes)
questions.explanation: "Do homework" — school tasks and assignments use "do".

questions.question: The pharmacist ___ a calculation error and gave the wrong dose.
questions.explanation: "Make an error" — producing mistakes uses "make".

questions.question: I ___ some cleaning this morning — the apartment looks much better.
questions.explanation: "Do some cleaning" — household activities use "do".

questions.question: She ___ a decision to study nursing after visiting the hospital. (did / made)
questions.explanation: "Make a decision" — creating a choice or plan uses "make".

questions.question: He ___ a lot of effort to learn English from scratch.
questions.explanation: "Make an effort" — producing effort uses "make".

questions.question: The children ___ a lot of noise in the corridor. (did / made)
questions.explanation: "Make noise" — producing sound uses "make".

questions.question: I need to ___ the grocery shopping before the weekend.
questions.explanation: "Do the shopping" — tasks and errands use "do".

title: Adverbs of Frequency — always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never

questions.question: She ___ takes the subway to work — every single day.
questions.explanation: "Always" = 100% of the time. She takes the subway every day without exception.

questions.question: I ___ eat at restaurants. I prefer to cook at home.
questions.explanation: "Rarely" = almost never. She eats out very infrequently.

questions.question: He is ___ late for work — his manager is very unhappy about it. (always / never / rarely)
questions.explanation: "Always late" — the adverb goes before the main verb but after "be": is always.

questions.question: I ___ check my email in the morning — it's part of my routine.
questions.explanation: "Usually" = most of the time, regularly as a habit.

questions.question: She ___ forgets her medication. She has a good reminder system.
questions.explanation: "Never" = 0% of the time. She never forgets — always remembers.

questions.question: We ___ go to the farmers market on Saturdays — maybe once a month. (sometimes / always / never)
questions.explanation: "Sometimes" = occasionally, but not regularly. Goes before the main verb.

questions.question: Do you ___ walk to work or do you take the bus?
questions.explanation: "Ever" is used in questions: Do you ever walk? = At any time, do you walk?

questions.question: I have ___ been to a job interview in English before — this is my first time.
questions.explanation: "Never" in present perfect: have never been = zero experience of this.

questions.question: She ___ speaks English at work because all her colleagues are from different countries. (always / never / rarely)
questions.explanation: "Always" = every single time. Goes before the main verb: always speaks.

questions.question: He is ___ cheerful — even on difficult days, he stays positive.
questions.explanation: "Always cheerful" — adverb after "be" verb: is always.

questions.question: I ___ check the expiry date on food before I buy it. (always / never / sometimes)
questions.explanation: "Always check" — good habit. Adverb before main verb: always check.

questions.question: She ___ takes public transport — she usually drives.
questions.explanation: "Rarely takes" = almost never. But she usually drives — contrast.

questions.question: Do you ___ have problems understanding fast English speakers?
questions.explanation: All adverbs can be used in questions. "Often" = frequently.

questions.question: He is ___ available on weekends — he works seven days a week. (never / always / sometimes)
questions.explanation: "Never available" — he has no free time on weekends.

questions.question: Where does the adverb go in this sentence? "She ___goes ___ to bed ___before midnight."
options: usually can go in first or second position
questions.explanation: "Usually" typically goes before the main verb: She usually goes. But position can vary for emphasis.

questions.question: I ___ eat breakfast before work — I'm usually too tired to cook.
questions.explanation: "Rarely eat breakfast" — almost never, because of tiredness.

questions.question: They ___ forget to pay their bills on time. The landlord is frustrated. (always / never / rarely)
questions.explanation: "Always forget" — 100% of the time, a habitual bad habit.

questions.question: She is ___ nervous before medical appointments.
questions.explanation: "Sometimes nervous" — occasionally but not every time. After "be" verb.

questions.question: I ___ read the terms and conditions — they are too long! (never / always / usually)
questions.explanation: "Never read" — 0% of the time. A common honest admission!

questions.question: He ___ arrives five minutes early to every appointment.
questions.explanation: "Always arrives early" — consistent habit. Adverb before the main verb: always arrives.

title: Present Perfect vs Past Simple — When to Use Each

questions.question: I ___ to three job interviews this week and I have one more tomorrow.
questions.explanation: "This week" is still ongoing → present perfect: have been.

questions.question: She ___ her nursing degree in 2021 and started working immediately.
questions.explanation: "In 2021" is a finished specific time → past simple: completed.

questions.question: I ___ never worked a night shift before. This is my first time. (have / had / did)
questions.explanation: "Never" with no specific time → present perfect: have never worked.

questions.question: How long ___ you live in this neighbourhood?
questions.explanation: "Did you live" asks about a finished period. "Have you lived" asks about now.

questions.question: The landlord ___ just fixed the boiler — it should be working now.
questions.explanation: "Just" with present perfect for very recent completion: has just fixed.

questions.question: She ___ as a translator for ten years before she became a teacher. (worked / has worked / had worked)
questions.explanation: "Before she became" = both actions are finished → past simple: worked.

questions.question: ___ you ever had to negotiate a salary increase?
questions.explanation: "Ever" with life experience, no specific time → present perfect: Have you ever...?

questions.question: The company ___ three new branches since last year.
questions.explanation: "Since last year" continuing until now → present perfect: has opened.

questions.question: I ___ the report before my manager arrived at the office. (finished / have finished / had finished)
questions.explanation: Finishing happened before another past event → past perfect: had finished.

questions.question: He ___ in four different countries during his career.
questions.explanation: Life experience with no specific time → present perfect: has worked.

questions.question: When did you ___ your current job? (start / started / have started)
questions.explanation: "When did you start?" — "when" with past simple asks for a specific time.

questions.question: I ___ this neighbourhood for years — I know every street.
questions.explanation: Ongoing state from the past until now → present perfect: have known.

questions.question: Shakespeare ___ 37 plays during his lifetime.
questions.explanation: Shakespeare is dead — his life is finished → past simple: wrote.

questions.question: She ___ already left by the time I arrived at the meeting. (had / has / was)
questions.explanation: Leaving happened before arriving → past perfect: had already left.

questions.question: I ___ three cups of coffee today and it's only 9am.
questions.explanation: "Today" is still ongoing → present perfect: have drunk.

questions.question: The contract ___ in March and both parties signed it the same day. (was signed / has been signed / had been signed)
questions.explanation: "In March" is a specific finished past time → past simple passive: was signed.

questions.question: Have you ___ your tax return yet?
questions.explanation: Present perfect: have + past participle. "Yet" signals present perfect.

questions.question: I ___ to the US in 2023 and I still live here now. (moved / have moved / had moved)
questions.explanation: "In 2023" is a specific past time → past simple: moved. (Even though I still live here.)

questions.question: She ___ already applied for permanent residency — she did it last month.
questions.explanation: "Already" with present perfect: has already applied. The action is relevant now.

questions.question: By the time I ___ my driving test, I had practised for six months. (passed / have passed / had passed)
questions.explanation: "By the time I passed" = the main event in sequence. Past simple for the reference point.

title: Third Conditional — Past Regrets & Hypotheticals

questions.question: If I had applied earlier, I ___ the scholarship.
questions.explanation: Third conditional: if + past perfect, would have + past participle.

questions.question: If she ___ the contract carefully, she wouldn't have signed it. (read / had read / has read)
questions.explanation: Third conditional if clause: had + past participle.

questions.question: He would have got the promotion if he ___ harder.
questions.explanation: If clause in third conditional: had + past participle.

questions.question: If the doctor ___ the symptoms earlier, the patient would have recovered faster.
questions.explanation: Third conditional: had identified = unreal past condition.

questions.question: She ___ the apartment if the rent hadn't been so high. (would rent / would have rented / had rented)
questions.explanation: Third conditional result clause: would have + past participle.

questions.question: If they had checked the weather forecast, they ___ a raincoat.
questions.explanation: Third conditional result: would have brought.

questions.question: If he ___ the wrong bus, he wouldn't have been late for the interview. (didn't take / hadn't taken / hasn't taken)
questions.explanation: Third conditional negative if clause: hadn't taken.

questions.question: She would have passed the citizenship test if she ___ more time preparing.
questions.explanation: Third conditional: had spent = past hypothetical effort.

questions.question: If the flight ___ cancelled, we would have missed the wedding.
questions.explanation: Third conditional passive if clause: hadn't been cancelled.

questions.question: I ___ you if I had known you were in hospital. (would visit / would have visited / had visited)
questions.explanation: Third conditional result clause: would have + past participle.

questions.question: If she ___ the job offer, she would have moved to Singapore.
questions.explanation: Third conditional if clause: had accepted.

questions.question: He ___ so stressed if he had planned ahead. (wouldn't be / wouldn't have been / hadn't been)
questions.explanation: Third conditional negative result: wouldn't have been.

questions.question: What would you have done if you ___ your passport at the airport?
questions.explanation: Third conditional: had lost = past hypothetical situation.

questions.question: If the landlord ___ us properly, we wouldn't have moved out.
questions.explanation: Third conditional if clause: had treated.

questions.question: She ___ to the doctor sooner if she had known it was serious. (would go / would have gone / had gone)
questions.explanation: Result clause: would have + past participle.

questions.question: If he ___ the instructions, the machine wouldn't have broken.
questions.explanation: Third conditional: had followed = what didn't happen in the past.

questions.question: If I ___ about the closing time, I wouldn't have made the trip. (knew / had known / know)
questions.explanation: Third conditional if clause: had known.

questions.question: The project ___ on time if the team had communicated better.
questions.explanation: Third conditional result: would have finished.

questions.question: If she ___ the email, she would have known about the meeting. (received / had received / has received)
questions.explanation: Third conditional if clause: had received.

questions.question: He would have become a citizen sooner if he ___ the language test earlier.
questions.explanation: Third conditional: had passed = past hypothetical action.

title: Modal Verbs — Deduction & Probability

questions.question: She's been working for 14 hours. She ___ be exhausted.
questions.explanation: "Must be" for near-certain logical deduction from the evidence.

questions.question: He ___ be a local — he has no idea where the post office is.
questions.explanation: "Can't be" for near-certain negative deduction — impossible given the evidence.

questions.question: I left my umbrella somewhere — I ___ have left it on the bus. (might / must / can't)
questions.explanation: "Might have left" — uncertain past possibility.

questions.question: Her English is perfect. She ___ have studied abroad for years.
questions.explanation: "Must have studied" — strong deduction from her level of English.

questions.question: He ___ have submitted the application — it was due yesterday and he always meets deadlines.
questions.explanation: "Must have submitted" — logical deduction from his reliable character.

questions.question: She ___ have taken the wrong exit — she's been driving for an hour on a 20-minute route. (must / might / can't)
questions.explanation: "Must have taken" — near-certain deduction from the time.

questions.question: That ___ be him calling — he's in a meeting all day.
questions.explanation: "Can't be him" — we are nearly certain it's impossible.

questions.question: I ___ have misread the address. Let me check the letter again.
questions.explanation: "Might have misread" — uncertain past possibility about a mistake.

questions.question: He knows every regulation by heart. He ___ have worked in immigration for years. (must / might / can't)
questions.explanation: "Must have worked" — strong deduction from his detailed knowledge.

questions.question: She ___ have received the letter — it was sent to the wrong address.
questions.explanation: "Can't have received" — logically impossible if sent to wrong address.

questions.question: There's smoke coming from the kitchen. Someone ___ be cooking. (must / might / can't)
questions.explanation: "Must be cooking" — near-certain present deduction from the smoke.

questions.question: I'm not sure if she got my message. She ___ not have seen it yet.
questions.explanation: "Might not have seen" — uncertain past possibility.

questions.question: He passed the bar exam on his first try. He ___ be a very dedicated student.
questions.explanation: "Must be" — logical conclusion from an impressive achievement.

questions.question: She looks completely calm. She ___ have done many presentations before. (must / might / can't)
questions.explanation: "Must have done" — strong deduction from her confident appearance.

questions.question: That ___ be the right clinic — this building is a car park.
questions.explanation: "Can't be" — we are certain from the visible evidence.

questions.question: She didn't answer her phone. She ___ be on the underground.
questions.explanation: "Might be on the underground" — one possible explanation.

questions.question: He ___ have forgotten the meeting — he confirmed it twice this morning. (must / might / can't)
questions.explanation: "Can't have forgotten" — almost impossible given he confirmed it.

questions.question: She knows everyone in the office by name. She ___ have worked here for a long time.
questions.explanation: "Must have worked" — strong deduction from her familiarity with everyone.

questions.question: I'm not sure why the payment was rejected. There ___ be an issue with the card. (must / might / can't)
questions.explanation: "Might be an issue" — uncertain, one possible explanation.

questions.question: He completed a three-year course in one year. He ___ be extremely talented.
questions.explanation: "Must be extremely talented" — the only logical conclusion.

title: Relative Clauses — who, which, that, where, whose, when

questions.question: The immigration officer ___ processed my application was very helpful.
questions.explanation: "Who" for people in relative clauses.

questions.question: The building ___ I work is ten minutes from the nearest subway.
questions.explanation: "Where" for places: the building where I work.

questions.question: The company ___ offered me the job has offices in five countries. (who / which / where)
questions.explanation: "Which" or "that" for organisations/things: the company which offered.

questions.question: She is the colleague ___ helped me fill in the tax forms.
questions.explanation: "Who" for people: the colleague who helped me.

questions.question: I remember the day ___ I got my first pay cheque in the US.
questions.explanation: "When" for times in relative clauses: the day when something happened.

questions.question: The lawyer ___ I consulted specialises in immigration law. (who / which / whose)
questions.explanation: "Who" for people: the lawyer who I consulted.

questions.question: The apartment ___ I rented last year was in a very noisy area.
questions.explanation: "Which" or "that" for things: the apartment which I rented.

questions.question: She works for a manager ___ son also works at the same company.
questions.explanation: "Whose" for possession: a manager whose son = the manager's son.

questions.question: The clinic ___ I go for check-ups is open on weekends. (where / which / who)
questions.explanation: "Where" for places: the clinic where I go.

questions.question: That was the year ___ the economy collapsed and unemployment rose sharply.
questions.explanation: "When" for years and time periods: the year when something happened.

questions.question: He has a colleague ___ speaks five languages and helps with translation. (who / which / where)
questions.explanation: "Who" for people: a colleague who speaks five languages.

questions.question: The reason ___ she left the job was the long commute.
questions.explanation: "Why" for reasons: the reason why she left.

questions.question: The contract ___ we signed had several clauses I didn't notice.
questions.explanation: "Which" or "that" for things: the contract which we signed.

questions.question: The neighbourhood ___ she grew up has changed completely. (where / which / who)
questions.explanation: "Where" for places: the neighbourhood where she grew up.

questions.question: She is a doctor ___ patients come from all over the country.
questions.explanation: "Whose" for possession: a doctor whose patients = the doctor's patients.

questions.question: The form ___ you need to fill in is available at the front desk.
questions.explanation: "That" or "which" for things: the form that you need.

questions.question: I remember the time ___ we had to queue for six hours at the embassy. (when / where / which)
questions.explanation: "When" for times: the time when we had to queue.

questions.question: The pharmacist ___ explained the dosage was very patient and clear.
questions.explanation: "Who" for people: the pharmacist who explained.

questions.question: The organisation ___ she volunteers for helps newly arrived immigrants. (which / who / where)
questions.explanation: "Which" for organisations: the organisation which she volunteers for.

questions.question: I live near the park ___ the community events are held every summer.
questions.explanation: "Where" for places: the park where events are held.

title: Gerunds & Infinitives — -ing vs to

questions.question: I enjoy ___ to different cultures and trying new food.
questions.explanation: "Enjoy" is always followed by a gerund (-ing): enjoy being exposed.

questions.question: She decided ___ her career and go back to university.
questions.explanation: "Decide" is followed by an infinitive (to + verb): decided to change.

questions.question: He admitted ___ the wrong medication to the patient. (giving / to give / give)
questions.explanation: "Admit" is followed by a gerund: admitted giving.

questions.question: They hope ___ permanent residency within the next two years.
questions.explanation: "Hope" is followed by an infinitive: hope to obtain.

questions.question: She managed ___ a job in her field within a month of arriving. (finding / to find / find)
questions.explanation: "Manage" is followed by an infinitive: managed to find.

questions.question: I miss ___ my family back home — video calls are not the same.
questions.explanation: "Miss" is followed by a gerund: miss seeing.

questions.question: He refused ___ the unfair clause in the contract.
questions.explanation: "Refuse" is followed by an infinitive: refused to sign.

questions.question: They suggested ___ a translator for the legal meeting. (hiring / to hire / hire)
questions.explanation: "Suggest" is followed by a gerund: suggested hiring.

questions.question: I kept ___ the instructions but couldn't understand them.
questions.explanation: "Keep" is followed by a gerund: kept reading.

questions.question: She promised ___ the lease agreement before signing anything.
questions.explanation: "Promise" is followed by an infinitive: promised to review.

questions.question: I don't mind ___ overtime occasionally if I'm compensated. (working / to work / work)
questions.explanation: "Don't mind" is followed by a gerund: don't mind working.

questions.question: He stopped ___ fast food after his doctor warned him about his health.
questions.explanation: "Stopped eating" = he was eating it and then quit. Different from "stopped to eat".

questions.question: I remember ___ that form but I can't find it anywhere now. (submitting / to submit / submit)
questions.explanation: "Remember + gerund" = I have a memory of doing it in the past.

questions.question: She tried ___ the customer service line but no one answered.
questions.explanation: "Tried to call" = she attempted it (but failed). "Tried calling" = she experimented with it.

questions.question: They avoid ___ on the highway during rush hour. (driving / to drive / drive)
questions.explanation: "Avoid" is followed by a gerund: avoid driving.

questions.question: I regret ___ you that your application has been unsuccessful.
questions.explanation: "Regret + to infinitive" in formal announcements — I am sorry to tell you this now.

questions.question: She pretended ___ the language barrier to avoid a difficult conversation. (not to understand / not understanding / understand not)
questions.explanation: "Pretend" + infinitive: pretended not to understand.

questions.question: He agreed ___ an extra shift to cover a colleague who was sick.
questions.explanation: "Agree" is followed by an infinitive: agreed to do.

questions.question: I can't help ___ nervous every time I have to speak English on the phone. (feeling / to feel / feel)
questions.explanation: "Can't help" is followed by a gerund: can't help feeling.

questions.question: She considered ___ to another city for better job opportunities.
questions.explanation: "Consider" is followed by a gerund: considered moving.

title: Wish & If Only — Regrets and Desires

questions.question: I wish I ___ better English when I first arrived — it would have been so much easier.
questions.explanation: "Wish + past perfect" for past regrets: wish I had spoken.

questions.question: She wishes she ___ closer to her family — she gets very lonely.
questions.explanation: "Wish + past simple" for present unreal wishes: wishes she lived closer.

questions.question: If only I ___ about the visa requirements before I booked the flight! (knew / had known / know)
questions.explanation: "If only + past perfect" for strong past regrets.

questions.question: I wish my landlord ___ fix things when they break — it takes weeks!
questions.explanation: "Wish + would" for annoying habits or behaviour we want to change.

questions.question: She wishes she ___ drive. It would make life much easier here. (can / could / would)
questions.explanation: "Wish + could" for wishing you had an ability you don't have.

questions.question: If only he ___ the job offer — it was a great opportunity.
questions.explanation: "If only + past perfect" for strong past regrets.

questions.question: I wish the public transport here ___ more reliable — I'm always late.
questions.explanation: "Wish + were" for present state wishes: wishes it were more reliable.

questions.question: If only she ___ the contract more carefully before signing. (read / had read / reads)
questions.explanation: "If only + past perfect" for past regrets about what didn't happen.

questions.question: He wishes people ___ speak more slowly — he still finds fast English difficult.
questions.explanation: "Wish + would" for wanting others to change their behaviour.

questions.question: I wish I ___ taken more time to prepare for the citizenship interview.
questions.explanation: "Wish + past perfect" for past regrets: wish I had taken.

questions.question: If only there ___ more affordable housing in this city. (is / was / were)
questions.explanation: "If only + were" for present unreal wishes about situations.

questions.question: She wishes she ___ saved more money before moving abroad.
questions.explanation: "Wish + past perfect" for past financial regrets.

questions.question: I wish my neighbours ___ make so much noise at night — I can't sleep.
questions.explanation: "Wish + wouldn't" for annoying repeated behaviour.

questions.question: If only I ___ the language before I moved — everything would have been easier. (learned / had learned / learn)
questions.explanation: "If only + past perfect" for strong regrets about the past.

questions.question: He wishes his English ___ good enough for a promotion right now.
questions.explanation: "Wish + were" for present wishes: wishes his English were good enough now.

questions.question: I wish I ___ started studying English much earlier in life.
questions.explanation: "Wish + past perfect" for a regret about a past decision.

questions.question: If only she ___ stop checking her phone during every conversation. (will / would / could)
questions.explanation: "If only + would" for strong wishes about annoying behaviour.

questions.question: He wishes he ___ speak to the hiring manager in person — the phone interview was difficult.
questions.explanation: "Wish + could" for wishing you had an ability or opportunity.

questions.question: I wish the process ___ quicker — waiting six months for a decision is very stressful. (is / was / were)
questions.explanation: "Wish + were" for present unreal wishes about situations.

questions.question: If only I ___ taken better notes at the orientation — I've forgotten everything.
questions.explanation: "If only + past perfect" for strong past regrets.

title: Causative Have & Get — Have Something Done

questions.question: She ___ her documents translated by a professional before the visa interview.
questions.explanation: "Had her documents translated" = causative have. Had + object + past participle.

questions.question: I need to ___ my references checked before submitting the job application.
questions.explanation: "Get something done" = causative: get + object + past participle.

questions.question: He ___ his eyes tested and discovered he needed glasses. (had his eyes tested / tested his eyes / got tested his eyes)
questions.explanation: Causative past: had + object + past participle.

questions.question: She ___ her resume reviewed by a career counsellor before applying.
questions.explanation: "Had her resume reviewed" = someone else reviewed it for her.

questions.question: I need to ___ my work permit renewed next month. (have my work permit renewed / renew my work permit / get renewed my work permit)
questions.explanation: Causative have: have + object + past participle.

questions.question: We ___ our apartment professionally cleaned before the landlord's inspection.
questions.explanation: Past causative: had + object + past participle.

questions.question: You should ___ your contract checked by a lawyer before you sign it.
questions.explanation: "Get something checked" — causative with get + object + past participle.

questions.question: She ___ her qualifications assessed by the official recognition body. (had her qualifications assessed / assessed her qualifications / got assessed her qualifications)
questions.explanation: Causative have: had + object (her qualifications) + past participle (assessed).

questions.question: He ___ his criminal background check done before starting the new job.
options: had his criminal background check done
has done his criminal background check
got done his criminal background check
questions.explanation: Causative: had + object + past participle.

questions.question: Where do you ___ your medical check-ups done?
questions.explanation: "Have something done" = someone else does it for you at a place you choose.

questions.question: I'm going to ___ my tax return prepared by an accountant this year. (have my tax return prepared / prepare my tax return / get my tax return prepare)
questions.explanation: Future causative: going to have + object + past participle.

questions.question: She ___ her official documents apostilled at the government office.
questions.explanation: Past causative: had + object + past participle.

questions.question: They are going to ___ the office repainted over the weekend.
questions.explanation: "Get something done" — future causative arrangement.

questions.question: You should ___ your blood pressure checked regularly if you have a desk job. (have your blood pressure checked / check your blood pressure / get checked your blood pressure)
questions.explanation: Advice with causative: should have + object + past participle.

questions.question: He ___ his work visa sponsored by his new employer.
questions.explanation: Causative: had his work visa sponsored = the employer sponsored it for him.

questions.question: I need to ___ my degree certificate verified by the university.
questions.explanation: "Get something verified" — causative with get.

questions.question: She ___ her hair cut and coloured at a new salon downtown. (had her hair cut and coloured / cut and coloured her hair / got her hair cutting)
