questions.answer: had her hair cut and coloured
questions.explanation: Causative: had + object + two past participles.

questions.question: How often do you ___ your car serviced?
questions.explanation: "Have your car serviced" = you take it to a mechanic.

questions.question: They are having the entire building ___ next year. (renovated / renovating / to renovate)
questions.explanation: Causative: are having + object + past participle. The object (building) receives the action.

questions.question: She ___ her medical records translated into English for the new doctor.
questions.explanation: Causative past: had + object + past participle.

title: Connectors — Contrast, Result, Addition & Purpose

questions.question: She moved to a new city ___ finding better job opportunities.
questions.explanation: "In order to" + infinitive expresses purpose: she moved in order to find better opportunities.

questions.question: He studied hard. ___, he passed the professional exam with top marks.
questions.explanation: "As a result" introduces the consequence of his studying.

questions.question: She took extra English classes ___ improve her chances of promotion. (so that / in order to / despite)
questions.explanation: "In order to" + infinitive expresses purpose.

questions.question: ___ the high cost of living, she decided to stay in the city for career reasons.
questions.explanation: "Despite" + noun/gerund for concession: despite the high cost.

questions.question: The job pays well. ___, the working hours are very long.
questions.explanation: "However" introduces a contrasting negative point.

questions.question: She saved money for three years ___ she could afford the visa fees. (so that / in order to / despite)
questions.explanation: "So that" + clause expresses purpose: she saved so that she could pay.

questions.question: The training was useful. ___, it could have been shorter.
questions.explanation: "Nevertheless" = despite this, showing contrast with a concession.

questions.question: He speaks English and Spanish. ___, he can communicate with most of the team.
questions.explanation: "Therefore" shows the logical result of speaking two languages.

questions.question: ___ having no formal qualifications, she built a very successful business. (Despite / Although / However)
questions.explanation: "Despite" + gerund/noun phrase for concession: despite having no qualifications.

questions.question: The position requires experience. ___, strong language skills are essential.
questions.explanation: "Furthermore" adds another requirement on top of what was already said.

questions.question: She works two jobs ___ support her family back home. (so that / in order to / despite)
questions.explanation: "In order to" + infinitive expresses purpose.

questions.question: ___ she had little experience, she impressed the panel with her enthusiasm.
questions.explanation: "Although" introduces a concession with a verb clause.

questions.question: The salary is competitive. ___, the benefits package is excellent.
questions.explanation: "Moreover" adds another positive point.

questions.question: He took a language course ___ he could communicate better with clients. (so that / in order to / despite)
questions.explanation: "So that" + could/would expresses purpose with a clause.

questions.question: The process is complicated. ___, many people complete it successfully every year.
questions.explanation: "Nevertheless" = despite the complexity, success is still common.

questions.question: She missed the application deadline. ___, her documents were incomplete.
questions.explanation: "Moreover" adds another problem on top of the first.

questions.question: ___ the language barrier, she managed to build strong relationships at work. (Despite / Although / However)
questions.explanation: "Despite" + noun phrase: despite the language barrier.

questions.question: She practises English every day ___ she can communicate more naturally at work.
questions.explanation: "So that" + can/could for purpose in the future or present.

questions.question: The job is demanding. ___, she finds it very rewarding. (However / Therefore / Moreover)
questions.explanation: "However" introduces a contrasting positive point.

questions.question: He studied accounting ___ being able to manage the company finances himself.
questions.explanation: "In order to" + gerund or infinitive expresses purpose.

title: Quantifiers — all, both, neither, either, each, every, none

questions.question: ___ of the two job offers was perfect — one paid well but had no benefits, the other had benefits but paid poorly.
questions.explanation: "Neither" = not one and not the other (negative for two things).

questions.question: ___ candidate impressed the hiring panel — they were all exceptional.
questions.explanation: "Every candidate" — singular countable noun after "every".

questions.question: You can apply ___ online or in person — the result is the same. (either / neither / both)
questions.explanation: "Either...or" = one option or the other. Both are acceptable choices.

questions.question: ___ of my qualifications were recognised here — I had to retrain completely.
questions.explanation: "None of my qualifications" = not any of them (for a group of more than two).

questions.question: ___ sides of the argument have some validity — it's a complex issue.
questions.explanation: "Both sides" = the two sides together, and they both have merit.

questions.question: She has worked in ___ the public and private sectors. (both / all / either)
questions.explanation: "Both the public and private sectors" = two sectors, she worked in each.

questions.question: ___ employee is required to complete the safety training.
questions.explanation: "Every employee" — singular noun, meaning all of them individually.

questions.question: ___ of us knew about the policy change — the manager forgot to inform anyone.
questions.explanation: "None of us" = not any of us (group of three or more).

questions.question: ___ the staff agreed with the decision — it was unanimous. (All / Both / Every)
questions.explanation: "All the staff" = the entire group. "All" with plural nouns.

questions.question: She applied for ___ positions — the junior and the senior role — and got an offer for both.
questions.explanation: "Both positions" = exactly two things, and she applied for each.

questions.question: ___ department has its own budget and reports directly to the CEO. (Every / All / Both)
questions.explanation: "Every department" — singular noun, considering each one individually.

questions.question: ___ of the three candidates was impressive in different ways.
questions.explanation: "Each of the three" considers them individually. "Both" is only for two.

questions.question: I contacted ___ the embassy and the consulate — ___ of them could help me.
questions.explanation: "Both" = I contacted the two of them; "neither" = not one and not the other could help.

questions.question: ___ member of the team received a performance bonus. (Every / All / Both)
questions.explanation: "Every member" — singular countable noun after "every".

questions.question: She speaks ___ English and Burmese at home — her children are growing up bilingual.
questions.explanation: "Both English and Burmese" = two languages, she speaks them both.

questions.question: ___ of the offices have been renovated — they both look brand new.
questions.explanation: "Both of the offices" = exactly two offices, and both have been renovated.

questions.question: ___ the applications were reviewed within 48 hours. (All / Every / Both)
questions.explanation: "All the applications" = the entire group of applications.

questions.question: You can pay ___ by card or cash — whichever is easier for you.
questions.explanation: "Either by card or cash" = one option or the other is acceptable.

questions.question: ___ of the branches offer the same services. (All / Both / Every)
questions.explanation: "All of the branches" = every single one in the group.

questions.question: ___ option is risk-free — you need to weigh up the advantages and disadvantages of each.
questions.explanation: "Neither option is risk-free" = not one and not the other is completely safe.

title: Reported Speech — Questions, Commands & Tense Changes

questions.question: "Where do you see yourself in five years?" → The interviewer asked where I ___ myself in five years.
questions.explanation: Reported question: present simple → past simple. Word order becomes normal.

questions.question: "Don't forget to bring your references." → She reminded me ___ my references.
questions.explanation: Reported reminder: reminded me + not to + base verb.

questions.question: "Can you start next Monday?" → She asked if I ___ start the following Monday. (can / could / would)
questions.explanation: "Can" → "could" in reported questions.

questions.question: "I will contact you by Friday." → He promised he ___ contact me by Friday.
questions.explanation: "Will" → "would" in reported speech.

questions.question: "Are you currently employed?" → She asked me if I ___ currently employed. (am / was / were)
questions.explanation: "Am" → "was" in reported questions.

questions.question: "I have been working here for three years." → She said she ___ there for three years.
questions.explanation: Present perfect continuous → past perfect continuous in reported speech.

questions.question: "Please sign the form before you leave." → She asked me ___ the form before I left. (sign / to sign / signing)
questions.explanation: Reported request: asked me + to + base verb.

questions.question: "What salary are you expecting?" → He asked what salary I ___.
questions.explanation: Present continuous → past continuous in reported questions.

questions.question: "I can't attend the meeting tomorrow." → She said she ___ attend the meeting the next day.
questions.explanation: "Can't" → "couldn't" in reported speech.

questions.question: "Have you worked with international clients before?" → He asked if I ___ with international clients before. (have worked / had worked / worked)
questions.explanation: Present perfect → past perfect in reported questions.

questions.question: "Don't use your personal phone during work hours." → The manager told us ___ our personal phones during work hours.
questions.explanation: Reported prohibition: told us + not to + base verb.

questions.question: "Why did you leave your last job?" → She asked me why I ___ my last job. (left / had left / leave)
questions.explanation: Past simple → past perfect in reported questions: had left.

questions.question: "We will offer you the position." → They told me they ___ offer me the position.
questions.explanation: "Will" → "would" in reported speech.

questions.question: "How long have you been living in this country?" → She asked how long I ___ in the country.
questions.explanation: Present perfect continuous → past perfect continuous in reported questions.

questions.question: "Make sure you read the employee handbook." → The HR manager told me ___ sure I read the employee handbook. (make / to make / making)
questions.explanation: Reported instruction: told me + to + base verb.

questions.question: "I must submit the report by tomorrow." → He said he ___ submit the report by the next day.
questions.explanation: "Must" → "had to" in reported speech.

questions.question: "Do you have any questions for us?" → She asked if I ___ any questions for them. (have / had / did have)
questions.explanation: "Do you have" → "if I had" in reported questions.

questions.question: "I'm sorry I'm late — the traffic was terrible." → He apologised ___ late, explaining that the traffic had been terrible.
questions.explanation: "Apologise for + gerund": apologised for being late.

questions.question: "You may take a break whenever you need." → The supervisor said I ___ take a break whenever I needed. (may / might / could)
questions.explanation: "May" → "might" in reported speech for permission.

questions.question: "What time does the interview start?" → She asked what time the interview ___.
questions.explanation: Present simple → past simple in reported questions.

title: Passive Voice — Advanced Forms & Uses

questions.question: The visa application ___ currently ___ reviewed by the immigration office.
questions.explanation: Present continuous passive: is being + past participle = in progress right now.

questions.question: The contract ___ already ___ signed by both parties.
questions.explanation: Present perfect passive: has been + past participle = completed recently.

questions.question: The new policy ___ announced next week. (will be / is being / has been)
questions.explanation: Future passive: will be + past participle.

questions.question: By the time she arrived, all the forms ___ already ___ processed.
questions.explanation: Past perfect passive: had been + past participle = completed before another past event.

questions.question: The application must ___ submitted with all supporting documents.
questions.explanation: Modal passive: must + be + past participle.

questions.question: All staff ___ required to complete a background check before starting. (are / were / have been)
questions.explanation: Present simple passive: are + past participle for current requirements.

questions.question: The building ___ ___ renovated for the past three months.
questions.explanation: Present perfect passive: has been + past participle.

questions.question: The new employees ___ being trained on the system right now.
questions.explanation: Present continuous passive: are being + past participle.

questions.question: The report should ___ submitted before the end of the week. (be / been / being)
questions.explanation: Modal passive: should + be + past participle.

questions.question: By next month, the new regulations ___ ___ implemented.
questions.explanation: Future perfect passive: will have been + past participle.

questions.question: Candidates ___ notified of the results within five working days. (will be / are being / have been)
questions.explanation: Future passive: will be + past participle.

questions.question: She ___ told about the change in policy before anyone else.
questions.explanation: Past simple passive: was + past participle.

questions.question: The documents need ___ verified by an authorised officer.
questions.explanation: After "need to", the passive is: need to be + past participle.

questions.question: The mistake ___ noticed until the report was already published. (wasn't / hadn't been / isn't)
questions.explanation: Past simple passive negative: wasn't + past participle.

questions.question: Her qualifications ___ being assessed by the recognition board at the moment.
questions.explanation: Present continuous passive: are being + past participle.

questions.question: This form ___ completed in black ink only.
questions.explanation: Modal passive: must be + past participle.

questions.question: By the time the meeting started, all the materials ___ already distributed. (were / had been / have been)
questions.explanation: Past perfect passive: had been + past participle = completed before another past event.

questions.question: The new branch ___ opened by the end of the year.
questions.explanation: Future passive: will be + past participle.

questions.question: The application ___ rejected because of missing documents. (was / is / had been)
questions.explanation: Past simple passive: was + past participle for a completed past action.

questions.question: All employees ___ informed of any changes to the schedule in advance.
questions.explanation: Modal passive: must be + past participle.

title: Mixed Tenses — Choosing the Right Tense

questions.question: By the time I ___ my degree, I will have been studying for four years.
questions.explanation: "By the time I finish" = future time clause uses present simple, not will.

questions.question: She ___ for the company since she graduated — over eight years now.
questions.explanation: Present perfect continuous for a situation ongoing from the past until now.

questions.question: When I arrived at the office, everyone ___ already left for the day. (had / has / was)
questions.explanation: Past perfect: everyone had already left before I arrived.

questions.question: I ___ the report when my colleague called and asked me to change everything.
questions.explanation: Past continuous for action in progress when interrupted: was finishing.

questions.question: By next December, she ___ in the US for exactly ten years.
questions.explanation: Future perfect for completion by a future time: will have lived.

questions.question: He ___ three countries before he finally settled in Canada. (lived in / had lived in / has lived in)
questions.explanation: Before settling = both finished, but living came first → past perfect.

questions.question: At this time tomorrow, I ___ in my first meeting at the new job.
questions.explanation: Future continuous for action in progress at a specific future time.

questions.question: She ___ always wanted to work in healthcare — it's her passion.
questions.explanation: Present perfect with "always" for something true from the past until now: has always wanted.

questions.question: I ___ for the bus for 40 minutes when it finally arrived. (waited / was waiting / had been waiting)
questions.explanation: Duration of waiting before a past event → past perfect continuous.

questions.question: She ___ the company for two years before she was offered a permanent contract.
questions.explanation: Past simple for a single completed past action with a clear result.

questions.question: Don't call me at 2pm — I ___ a job interview then. (have / will have / will be having)
questions.explanation: Future continuous for action in progress at a specific future time.

questions.question: I ___ this city for years, but I still discover new places.
questions.explanation: Present perfect for an ongoing situation from the past until now.

questions.question: She ___ the form three times before the system finally accepted it.
questions.explanation: Past simple for a repeated completed action in the past.

questions.question: By the time you read this email, I ___ already left the office. (will have / would have / had)
questions.explanation: Future perfect: will have + past participle = completed before a future reference point.

questions.question: He ___ about the job offer when I called — he sounded very excited.
questions.explanation: Past perfect with "just" for something that happened very recently before another past event.

questions.question: I ___ my application unless I hear back from them by Friday.
questions.explanation: "Will withdraw" = future decision contingent on a condition.

questions.question: She ___ English for two years before she felt confident enough to apply for jobs. (studied / had been studying / has been studying)
questions.explanation: Duration of study before a past result → past perfect continuous.

questions.question: He ___ the training before he started the actual job — he was very prepared.
questions.explanation: Completing training happened before starting the job → past perfect.

questions.question: This time next year, I ___ for this company for five years. (work / will work / will have been working)
questions.explanation: Future perfect continuous: will have been + verb-ing for duration up to a future point.

questions.question: I ___ the contract carefully before I sign it.
questions.explanation: "Will read" for a firm future intention.

title: Emphasis — It was...that, What I need is...

questions.question: ___ the long commute that made her decide to find a new job.
questions.explanation: "It was...that" = cleft sentence for emphasis. Emphasises the reason.

questions.question: ___ I need is a clearer explanation of the visa requirements.
questions.explanation: "What I need is..." = wh-cleft for emphasis on what is needed.

questions.question: ___ the manager, not the HR team, who made the final decision. (It was / What was / There was)
questions.explanation: "It was...who" = cleft sentence emphasising the person responsible.

questions.question: ___ her dedication that got her the promotion, not her qualifications.
questions.explanation: "It was...that" emphasises the key factor (dedication, not qualifications).

questions.question: ___ really bothers me is the lack of communication in this office.
questions.explanation: "What bothers me is..." = wh-cleft to emphasise the source of frustration.

questions.question: ___ in 2023 that she finally got her permanent residency. (It was / What was / There was)
questions.explanation: "It was in 2023 that..." emphasises the specific time.

questions.question: ___ I find most challenging is adapting to a completely different work culture.
questions.explanation: "What I find most challenging is..." = wh-cleft for emphasis.

questions.question: ___ the incorrect address on the form that caused the delay.
questions.explanation: "It was...that" = cleft sentence identifying the cause of the problem.

questions.question: ___ surprised me was how quickly I adapted to the new environment. (What / It / That)
questions.explanation: "What surprised me was..." = wh-cleft emphasising the surprising thing.

questions.question: ___ the HR department that I should contact about my contract.
questions.explanation: "It is...that" = present cleft for emphasis on which department.

questions.question: ___ her positive attitude that made her stand out from other candidates. (It was / What was / That was)
questions.explanation: "It was...that" identifies the key differentiating factor.

questions.question: ___ I enjoyed most about the job was the variety of tasks.
questions.explanation: "What I enjoyed most was..." = wh-cleft for emphasis.

questions.question: ___ the lack of job security that worried her most about the offer.
questions.explanation: "It was...that" identifies the main concern.

questions.question: ___ really matters is that you have relevant experience, not just qualifications. (What / It / That)
questions.explanation: "What really matters is..." = wh-cleft for emphasis on importance.

questions.question: ___ her supervisor who recommended her for the advanced training programme.
questions.explanation: "It was...who" = cleft sentence emphasising who made the recommendation.

questions.question: ___ I struggled with most when I first arrived was the informal workplace culture.
questions.explanation: "What I struggled with was..." = wh-cleft for emphasis.

questions.question: ___ the networking event where she met her future employer. (It was / What was / There was)
questions.explanation: "It was...where" = cleft sentence identifying the place of the important event.

questions.question: ___ I appreciate about this country is the work-life balance.
questions.explanation: "What I appreciate is..." = wh-cleft for emphasis.

questions.question: ___ only after the second interview that she felt confident about getting the job. (It was / What was / That was)
questions.explanation: "It was only after...that" = cleft sentence with "only" for extra emphasis.

questions.question: ___ the support from her colleagues that helped her get through the difficult first year.
questions.explanation: "It was...that" identifies the crucial factor that helped.

title: Mixed Conditionals — Past & Present Combined

questions.question: If I had studied medicine, I ___ a doctor now.
questions.explanation: Mixed conditional: past condition (had studied) → present result (would be now).

questions.question: If she ___ more ambitious, she would have applied for the senior role.
questions.explanation: Present trait → past result: if she weren't so passive (now), she would have applied.

questions.question: If I had taken that job offer, I ___ living in Singapore now. (would be / will be / was)
questions.explanation: Past decision → present situation: would be living now.

questions.question: He would be in a much better financial position now if he ___ wasted so much money.
questions.explanation: Past action → present state: hadn't wasted (past perfect) → would be (now).

questions.question: If she ___ so stubborn, she would have been willing to compromise. (isn't / wasn't / weren't)
questions.explanation: Present personality trait → past result: if she weren't stubborn (now) → would have compromised.

questions.question: I would have applied for that scholarship if I ___ confident enough in my English.
questions.explanation: Present state → past result: if I were more confident (but I'm not) → would have applied.

questions.question: If he had built a stronger network earlier in his career, he ___ struggling to find work now. (wouldn't be / wouldn't have been / wasn't)
questions.explanation: Past action → present consequence: wouldn't be struggling now.

questions.question: She ___ fluent in English by now if she had practised consistently.
questions.explanation: Past habit → present state: would be fluent now.

questions.question: If I weren't so afraid of confrontation, I ___ negotiated a better salary. (would have / will have / had)
questions.explanation: Present trait → past missed opportunity: would have negotiated.

questions.question: If they had invested in training their staff, the company ___ in a stronger position today.
questions.explanation: Past investment decision → present company position: would be stronger now.

questions.question: He ___ so much stress now if he had learned to delegate earlier. (wouldn't have / wouldn't be experiencing / won't have)
questions.explanation: Past habit → present ongoing state: wouldn't be experiencing now.

questions.question: If she weren't so detail-oriented, she ___ the discrepancy in the contract.
questions.explanation: Present character trait → past result: wouldn't have noticed (but she did notice).

questions.question: I ___ such a strong accent now if I had practised pronunciation more seriously.
questions.explanation: Past practice → present state: wouldn't have such an accent now.

questions.question: If he had sought legal advice earlier, he ___ in this situation now. (wouldn't be / wouldn't have been / won't be)
questions.explanation: Past decision → present situation: wouldn't be in this situation now.

questions.question: She would have felt more confident at the interview if she ___ a naturally outgoing person.
questions.explanation: Present personality → past performance: if she were outgoing (but she isn't).

questions.question: If I had saved more consistently, I ___ able to afford my own apartment by now. (would be / would have been / will be)
questions.explanation: Past saving habit → present financial ability: would be able to afford now.

questions.question: He ___ promoted years ago if he were better at communicating his achievements.
questions.explanation: Present weakness → past missed opportunity: would have been promoted.

questions.question: If she had taken the internship seriously, she ___ working for that company now. (would be / would have been / will be)
questions.explanation: Past attitude → present employment situation: would be working there now.

questions.question: I wouldn't be so overwhelmed now if I ___ better boundaries with my manager from the start.
questions.explanation: Past action → present state: had set better boundaries → wouldn't be overwhelmed now.

questions.question: If he ___ more resilient, he would have handled the criticism better. (is / was / were)
questions.explanation: Present personality → past performance: if he were more resilient (but he isn't).

title: Inversion & Emphasis — Never have I, Not only...

questions.question: Never ___ such blatant discrimination in a workplace before.
questions.explanation: After "Never" at the start, inversion is required: Never have I witnessed.

questions.question: Not only ___ the deadline, but he also submitted incomplete work.
questions.explanation: "Not only" at the start requires subject-auxiliary inversion: Not only did he miss.

questions.question: Rarely ___ a candidate impress us so thoroughly in a first interview. (does / do / did)
questions.explanation: "Rarely" at the start triggers present inversion: Rarely does a candidate impress.

questions.question: Hardly ___ she started her presentation when the fire alarm went off.
questions.explanation: "Hardly...when" requires inversion: Hardly had she started.

questions.question: Little ___ that his casual remark would cause such serious consequences. (he knew / knew he / did he know)
questions.explanation: "Little" as a negative adverbial triggers inversion: Little did he know.

questions.question: No sooner ___ the agreement than one party tried to renegotiate the terms.
questions.explanation: "No sooner...than" requires past perfect inversion: No sooner had they signed.

questions.question: Only when she received the formal offer ___ she feel fully secure in her decision. (she did / did she / she had)
questions.explanation: "Only when" triggers inversion in the main clause: did she feel.

questions.question: Under no circumstances ___ personal client information with third parties.
questions.explanation: "Under no circumstances" requires inversion: should you share.

questions.question: So exhausted ___ she that she fell asleep during the meeting. (was / is / has been)
questions.explanation: "So + adjective" at the start triggers inversion: So exhausted was she.

questions.question: Not until she received the final pay cheque ___ she believe the contract dispute was truly over.
questions.explanation: "Not until" triggers inversion: did she believe.

questions.question: Seldom ___ a resignation letter cause such a ripple of shock throughout the organisation. (have / does / did)
questions.explanation: "Seldom" triggers present inversion: Seldom does a resignation letter cause.

questions.question: Not only ___ the salary increase denied, but her annual leave was also reduced.
questions.explanation: "Not only" inversion with passive: Not only was the salary increase denied.

questions.question: Only after weeks of mediation ___ the two sides reach a compromise. (they did / did they / they had)
questions.explanation: "Only after" triggers inversion in the result clause: did they reach.

questions.question: In no way ___ the company liable for losses incurred before the contract was signed.
questions.explanation: "In no way" triggers inversion: In no way is the company liable.

questions.question: Such ___ the pressure that three senior employees resigned in the same week. (was it / was / it was)
questions.explanation: "Such was..." — inversion with "such" for emphasis: Such was the pressure.

questions.question: Barely ___ she arrived when her manager handed her a stack of urgent tasks.
questions.explanation: "Barely" triggers inversion: Barely had she arrived.

questions.question: Only by remaining persistent ___ she eventually secure the funding she needed. (can / did / was)
questions.explanation: "Only by" triggers inversion: Only by remaining persistent did she eventually secure.

questions.question: At no point ___ the interviewer acknowledge the candidate's extensive experience.
questions.explanation: "At no point" triggers inversion: did he acknowledge.

questions.question: So effectively ___ she handle the complaint that the customer left completely satisfied. (she did / did she / has she)
questions.explanation: "So + adverb" triggers inversion: So effectively did she handle.

questions.question: Not once ___ she ask for acknowledgement despite consistently going above and beyond.
questions.explanation: "Not once" triggers inversion: Not once has she asked.

title: Advanced Passive — is said to be, reported to have

questions.question: The CEO ___ to be considering a major restructure of the organisation.
questions.explanation: "Is said to be" = people say he is — a distancing passive reporting structure.

questions.question: The new policy ___ to have significantly reduced staff turnover.
questions.explanation: "Is believed to have" — passive reporting verb + perfect infinitive for a past result.

questions.question: The company ___ to be facing serious allegations of misconduct. (is rumoured / rumours / rumoured)
questions.explanation: "Is rumoured to be" — passive reporting for unconfirmed information.

questions.question: Several employees ___ to have raised concerns about workplace safety before the incident.
questions.explanation: "Are known to have" — plural subject passive with perfect infinitive.

questions.question: The merger ___ to have been finalised without proper due diligence. (is claimed / claims / claimed)
questions.explanation: "Is claimed to have been finalised" — passive + perfect passive infinitive.

questions.question: Having ___ overlooked for promotion three times, she decided to look for opportunities elsewhere.
questions.explanation: "Having been overlooked" — perfect passive participle for a completed passive experience.

questions.question: The redundancies ___ reported to have affected over 200 employees across three departments.
questions.explanation: "Are reported to have affected" — present passive reporting + perfect infinitive.

questions.question: The whistleblower ___ to have leaked confidential documents to the press. (is alleged / alleges / alleged)
questions.explanation: "Is alleged to have leaked" — legal passive reporting structure.

questions.question: She is ___ to resign following the controversy over the financial irregularities.
questions.explanation: "Is expected to resign" — passive + infinitive for anticipated future action.

questions.question: The settlement ___ believed to be worth several million dollars, though neither side would confirm.
questions.explanation: "Is believed to be" — singular passive reporting verb for current belief.

questions.question: The investigation ___ said to be ongoing, with results expected within the month. (is / are / was)
questions.explanation: "Is said to be ongoing" — present passive reporting for a current situation.

questions.question: Measures ___ currently being taken to address the persistent gender pay gap within the sector.
questions.explanation: "Are being taken" — present continuous passive, plural subject.

questions.question: The regulation ___ due to be implemented by the end of the financial year. (is / are / was)
questions.explanation: "Is due to be implemented" — future passive with "due to".

questions.question: Having ___ denied access to the data, the auditors were unable to complete their report.
questions.explanation: "Having been denied" — perfect passive participle for a prior passive experience.

questions.question: The contract ___ understood to include a non-compete clause, though this was never explicitly stated. (is / are / was)
questions.explanation: "Is understood to include" — passive reporting for general assumption.

questions.question: The new legislation ___ thought to be the most significant reform the sector has seen in decades.
questions.explanation: "Is thought to be" — singular passive reporting for current assessment.

questions.question: She ___ known to be exceptionally thorough in her approach to compliance. (is / are / was)
questions.explanation: "Is known to be" — passive + infinitive for established reputation.

questions.question: The candidate ___ understood to have withdrawn their application due to a competing offer.
questions.explanation: "Is understood to have withdrawn" — passive reporting + perfect infinitive.

questions.question: Significant improvements ___ reported to have been made since the new management team took over. (are / is / was)
questions.explanation: "Are reported to have been made" — plural passive reporting + perfect passive infinitive.

questions.question: The directive ___ set to come into force on the first of next month.
questions.explanation: "Is set to come into force" — passive + infinitive for scheduled future action.

title: Subjunctive — It is essential that, I suggest that...

questions.question: It is essential that every employee ___ the code of conduct before signing.
questions.explanation: Subjunctive after "It is essential that": base form "read" for all subjects.

questions.question: The board recommended that she ___ an independent financial adviser before proceeding.
questions.explanation: "Recommend that" + subjunctive: base form "consult" (no -s even with she).

questions.question: It is vital that all disputes ___ resolved through the formal grievance procedure. (is / are / be)
questions.explanation: Passive subjunctive: "vital that" + base form "be resolved".

questions.question: I suggest that the contract ___ reviewed by a legal professional before you sign.
questions.explanation: "Suggest that" + passive subjunctive: base form "be reviewed".

questions.question: It is imperative that the company ___ full transparency in all financial disclosures. (maintains / maintain / maintaining)
questions.explanation: "Imperative that" + subjunctive base form: maintain (no -s).

questions.question: The committee insisted that the proposal ___ resubmitted with additional supporting evidence.
questions.explanation: "Insist that" + passive subjunctive: "be resubmitted".

questions.question: It is crucial that each applicant ___ given equal consideration regardless of background. (is / are / be)
questions.explanation: Passive subjunctive: "crucial that each applicant be given".

questions.question: The regulator requires that all transactions ___ recorded and auditable.
questions.explanation: "Require that" + subjunctive: base form "be recorded".

questions.question: The manager proposed that the team ___ their concerns through the proper channels. (raises / raise / raised)
questions.explanation: "Propose that" + subjunctive: "raise" (base form, no -s).

questions.question: It is advisable that you ___ independent legal advice before accepting any settlement offer.
questions.explanation: "Advisable that" + subjunctive: "seek" (base form).

questions.question: The policy stipulates that no information ___ disclosed without written consent. (is / are / be)
questions.explanation: "Stipulate that" + passive subjunctive: "be disclosed".

questions.question: She requested that all feedback ___ submitted in writing rather than verbally.
questions.explanation: "Request that" + passive subjunctive: "be submitted".

questions.question: It is mandatory that every staff member ___ a health and safety induction on their first day. (completes / complete / completing)
questions.explanation: "Mandatory that" + subjunctive: base form "complete" (no -s).

questions.question: The auditors demanded that all records ___ made available for inspection without delay.
questions.explanation: "Demand that" + passive subjunctive: "be made available".

questions.question: I recommend that she ___ the terms of the agreement with her union representative before signing. (discusses / discuss / discussing)
questions.explanation: "Recommend that" + subjunctive: base form "discuss" (no -s).

questions.question: It is important that the candidate ___ notified of the decision within five working days.
questions.explanation: Passive subjunctive: "important that the candidate be notified".

questions.question: The court ordered that the assets ___ frozen pending the outcome of the investigation. (are / were / be)
questions.explanation: "Order that" + passive subjunctive: "be frozen".

questions.question: The HR director suggested that a mediator ___ brought in to facilitate the negotiations.
questions.explanation: "Suggest that" + passive subjunctive: "be brought in".

questions.question: It is essential that no one ___ the office until the security sweep is complete. (leaves / leave / leaving)
questions.explanation: "Essential that no one" + subjunctive: base form "leave".

questions.question: The terms of the agreement specify that all payments ___ made in the agreed currency.
questions.explanation: "Specify that" + passive subjunctive: "be made".

title: Discourse Markers — nevertheless, consequently, furthermore

questions.question: The negotiation was tense. ___, both parties reached a compromise by the end of the day.
questions.explanation: "Nevertheless" = despite this. Shows a surprising positive result after a negative situation.

questions.question: She lacked formal qualifications. ___, her practical experience more than compensated.
questions.explanation: "However" introduces a contrasting point — her experience compensated for lack of qualifications.

questions.question: The company failed to meet its obligations. ___, the contract was terminated. (Consequently / Nevertheless / Furthermore)
questions.explanation: "Consequently" = as a result. Failure to meet obligations → contract terminated.

questions.question: The salary was lower than expected. ___, the benefits package was outstanding.
questions.explanation: "Nevertheless" — despite the salary issue, the benefits compensated.

questions.question: She is highly qualified. ___, she has extensive experience managing large teams.
questions.explanation: "Furthermore" adds another positive quality on top of the first.

questions.question: The report was submitted late. ___, the data it contained was incomplete. (Furthermore / However / Consequently)
questions.explanation: "Furthermore" adds another problem — two issues stacked.

questions.question: The organisation faced significant criticism. ___, it managed to maintain the trust of its core supporters.
questions.explanation: "Nevertheless" — despite criticism, they maintained support.

questions.question: Demand for the product declined sharply. ___, the company had to lay off 30% of its workforce.
questions.explanation: "As a result" connects cause (declining demand) to consequence (layoffs).

questions.question: The candidate had no relevant experience. ___, the panel decided to offer her the position based on her potential. (Nevertheless / Furthermore / Consequently)
questions.explanation: "Nevertheless" — despite the lack of experience, they offered the position.

questions.question: The new system is faster and more reliable. ___, it is significantly cheaper to maintain.
questions.explanation: "Moreover" adds another advantage: cheaper to maintain.

questions.question: The project ran significantly over budget. ___, key milestones were missed. (Furthermore / Nevertheless / However)
questions.explanation: "Furthermore" adds another failure — over budget AND missed milestones.

questions.question: Staff morale was low. ___, productivity remained surprisingly high.
questions.explanation: "Nevertheless" — despite low morale, productivity stayed high.

questions.question: The investigation revealed serious irregularities. ___, the regulator imposed substantial fines.
questions.explanation: "Consequently" = as a result of the irregularities, fines were imposed.

questions.question: The policy change was unpopular. ___, management pushed ahead with implementation. (Nevertheless / Furthermore / Consequently)
questions.explanation: "Nevertheless" — despite unpopularity, management proceeded.

questions.question: The team worked exceptionally hard. ___, the project was delivered ahead of schedule.
questions.explanation: "As a result" connects hard work to the early delivery.

questions.question: The location was inconvenient. ___, the rent was extremely reasonable.
questions.explanation: "However" contrasts the negative (inconvenient location) with a positive (reasonable rent).

questions.question: Several concerns were raised about the proposal. ___, the committee voted unanimously in favour. (Nevertheless / Furthermore / Consequently)
questions.explanation: "Nevertheless" — despite concerns, the vote was unanimous.

questions.question: The merger was approved by shareholders. ___, regulatory clearance is still required before it can proceed.
questions.explanation: "However" introduces a remaining obstacle despite shareholder approval.

questions.question: The candidate performed brilliantly at interview. ___, her references were outstanding. (Furthermore / Nevertheless / However)
questions.explanation: "Furthermore" adds another positive: outstanding references on top of interview performance.

questions.question: The initial offer was rejected. ___, negotiations continued and a deal was eventually reached.
questions.explanation: "Nevertheless" — despite rejection, negotiations continued and succeeded.

title: Participle Clauses — Having finished, Being tired, Not knowing

questions.question: ___ the report, she submitted it and went home.
questions.explanation: "Having finished" = after finishing. Perfect participle for a completed action before the main action.

questions.question: ___ exhausted after the long shift, the nurse made a small but consequential error.
questions.explanation: "Being exhausted" = because she was exhausted. Present participle for simultaneous cause.

questions.question: ___ what to say, he remained silent throughout the entire meeting. (Not knowing / Having not known / Being not known)
questions.explanation: "Not knowing" = because he didn't know. Negative participle for a simultaneous reason.

questions.question: ___ considerable experience in the field, she was able to identify the flaw immediately.
questions.explanation: "Having had considerable experience" = because she had experience. Perfect participle.

questions.question: The agreement ___ signed by all parties, the project could finally begin.
questions.explanation: "Having been signed" = after it was signed. Perfect passive participle.

questions.question: ___ from a different cultural background, she sometimes misread social cues in the workplace. (Coming / Having come / Come)
questions.explanation: "Coming from" = because she came from. Present participle for ongoing background reason.

questions.question: ___ the implications of the contract, she refused to sign without legal advice.
questions.explanation: Both "Realising" (simultaneous) and "Having realised" (prior realisation) are correct here.

questions.question: ___ told about the redundancies, the staff gathered to discuss their options.
questions.explanation: "Having been told" = after being informed. Perfect passive participle.

questions.question: ___ to speak the local language, she relied heavily on written communication. (Unable / Being unable / Having unable)
questions.explanation: "Being unable" = because she was unable. Present participle for reason.

questions.question: The terms ___ agreed upon, the lawyers began drafting the formal contract.
questions.explanation: "The terms having been agreed" = after the terms were agreed. Absolute participle clause.

questions.question: ___ in a multicultural environment, she developed strong cross-cultural communication skills. (Working / Having worked / Worked)
questions.explanation: "Having worked" emphasises the completed experience that led to her skills.

questions.question: ___ the risks involved, they decided to proceed with caution rather than abandon the project entirely.
questions.explanation: Both are correct — "Acknowledging" (simultaneous) and "Having acknowledged" (prior awareness).

questions.question: ___ the necessary qualifications, she was ineligible to apply for the position.
questions.explanation: "Not having the necessary qualifications" = because she didn't have them. Negative perfect participle.

questions.question: The deadline ___, the team worked through the night to finalise the proposal. (approaching / having approached / approached)
questions.explanation: "The deadline approaching" = as the deadline was approaching. Absolute participial clause.

questions.question: ___ to a senior role without sufficient preparation, he struggled to manage the additional responsibilities.
questions.explanation: "Having been promoted" = after being promoted. Perfect passive participle.

questions.question: ___ all available options, the management team decided that restructuring was unavoidable. (Considering / Having considered / Considered)
questions.explanation: "Having considered all options" = after considering. Shows prior deliberation.

questions.question: The complaint ___, the manager arranged a formal meeting with all parties involved.
questions.explanation: "The complaint having been received" = after the complaint was received. Absolute passive participle.

questions.question: ___ several times without success, she changed her approach entirely.
questions.explanation: "Having failed several times" = after failing. Shows prior completed experience.

questions.question: ___ the gravity of the situation, the director called an emergency board meeting. (Sensing / Having sensed / Both are correct)
questions.explanation: "Sensing" (in the moment) and "Having sensed" (prior realisation) are both grammatically correct.

questions.question: ___ the evidence objectively, it is difficult to support either side's position entirely.
questions.explanation: "Assessing the evidence objectively" = when you assess. Present participle for condition.

title: Advanced Modal Perfect — should have, could have, needn't have

questions.question: You ___ accepted that offer without reading the terms first. It was reckless.
questions.explanation: "Shouldn't have" = it was wrong to do it. Past criticism of an unwise action.

questions.question: She ___ handled the situation more diplomatically — the fallout was completely avoidable.
questions.explanation: "Could have" = it was possible but didn't happen. She had the ability but didn't use it.

questions.question: You ___ worked overtime — we had already met the quota for the week. (needn't have / shouldn't have / couldn't have)
questions.explanation: "Needn't have worked" = it was unnecessary. You did it but it wasn't needed.

questions.question: He ___ been aware of the policy — he had attended the briefing session.
questions.explanation: "Must have been aware" = near-certain deduction. He attended the briefing so he knew.

questions.question: She ___ resigned without securing alternative employment first — it was a huge risk.
questions.explanation: "Shouldn't have resigned" = past criticism. It was unwise to resign without a backup.

questions.question: We ___ printed all those copies — the meeting was cancelled. (needn't have / shouldn't have / couldn't have)
questions.explanation: "Needn't have printed" = unnecessary action. We did it but it turned out to not be needed.

questions.question: With better preparation, the team ___ achieved a much more favourable outcome in the negotiation.
questions.explanation: "Could have achieved" = it was possible with different preparation but didn't happen.

questions.question: I ___ pushed so hard for the promotion — the timing was clearly wrong.
questions.explanation: "Shouldn't have pushed" = past regret about an unwise decision.

questions.question: He ___ told us directly — sending a vague email only created confusion. (should have / could have / needn't have)
questions.explanation: "Should have told" = past criticism — it was the right thing to do but he didn't.

questions.question: You ___ worried so much about the presentation — it went brilliantly.
questions.explanation: "Needn't have worried" = the worry was unnecessary. Everything went well.

questions.question: She ___ disclosed that information in a public meeting — it was highly sensitive. (shouldn't have / needn't have / couldn't have)
questions.explanation: "Shouldn't have disclosed" = past criticism. It was wrong to share that information publicly.

questions.question: They ___ anticipated the backlash — the warning signs were obvious in hindsight.
questions.explanation: "Could have anticipated" = it was possible to foresee but they didn't.

questions.question: We ___ hired an external consultant — we already had the expertise in-house.
questions.explanation: "Needn't have hired" = it was unnecessary. We had the expertise already.

questions.question: I ___ kept a copy of every email — now I have no evidence of what was agreed. (should have / could have / needn't have)
questions.explanation: "Should have kept" = past regret about something important that wasn't done.

questions.question: She ___ handled a crisis of that magnitude — she had never managed one before.
questions.explanation: "Couldn't have handled" = it was beyond her capability at that point.

questions.question: You ___ rushed the application — there was still a week before the deadline.
questions.explanation: "Needn't have rushed" = it was unnecessary. There was still time.

questions.question: He ___ informed us sooner — the delay caused significant disruption. (should have / could have / needn't have)
questions.explanation: "Should have informed" = past criticism. The right action was to tell us earlier.

questions.question: With a little more patience, the conflict ___ been resolved without formal arbitration.
questions.explanation: "Could have been resolved" = it was possible but the right conditions weren't in place.

questions.question: She ___ apologised publicly — a private resolution would have sufficed. (needn't have / shouldn't have / couldn't have)
questions.explanation: "Needn't have apologised publicly" = the public apology was unnecessary.

questions.question: The company ___ communicated the changes more clearly — the confusion was entirely foreseeable.
questions.explanation: "Should have communicated" = past criticism. It was the right thing to do but wasn't done.

title: Hedging & Formal Language — It appears, tend to, is likely to

questions.question: It ___ that the new regulations will have a significant impact on small businesses.
questions.explanation: "It seems that" and "it appears that" are both correct hedging phrases for uncertain conclusions.

questions.question: The results ___ to suggest a strong correlation between stress levels and staff turnover.
questions.explanation: "The results appear to" and "the results seem to" are both correct for cautious academic language.

questions.question: Employees in high-pressure roles ___ to experience burnout more frequently. (tend / appear / seem)
questions.explanation: "Tend to" = generally, as a pattern. Used for observed tendencies rather than certainties.

questions.question: This approach ___ likely to produce more sustainable results over the long term.
questions.explanation: "Is likely to" = probably will. A hedging structure for cautious predictions.

questions.question: ___ that the organisation may need to reconsider its recruitment strategy entirely.
questions.explanation: All three — "It appears", "It seems" and "It would seem" — are correct hedging openers.

questions.question: The data ___ to indicate a gradual shift in consumer behaviour over the past decade. (tend / appears / seem)
questions.explanation: "The data appears to indicate" — "appears to" for cautious academic observation.

questions.question: There ___ to be a significant gap between the skills employers require and those graduates possess.
questions.explanation: "There appears to be" and "There seems to be" are both correct for hedging.

questions.question: Candidates from non-traditional backgrounds ___ to be overlooked despite strong qualifications.
questions.explanation: All three can be used: "tend to be overlooked" (pattern), "appear/seem to be" (observation).

questions.question: It ___ seem that a more flexible working policy could significantly improve retention rates. (would / could / should)
questions.explanation: "It would seem that" — "would" makes the hedging even more tentative and formal.

questions.question: The findings ___ to suggest that early intervention is considerably more effective than remedial action.
questions.explanation: All three work here. "Appear/seem to suggest" and "tend to suggest" express different nuances of caution.

questions.question: It ___ that employee wellbeing initiatives may be more cost-effective than previously assumed. (appears / tends / looks)
questions.explanation: "It appears that" — a formal hedging opener for cautious conclusions.

questions.question: This ___ unlikely to be the most efficient solution given the constraints of the current budget.
questions.explanation: "Is unlikely to be" = probably is not. A direct but hedged expression of doubt.

questions.question: People who work remotely ___ to report higher levels of job satisfaction but lower feelings of belonging.
questions.explanation: All three are correct. "Tend to" emphasises pattern; "appear/seem to" emphasises observation.

questions.question: It ___ plausible that the decline in applications is linked to the increased cost of living. (seems / tends / looks)
questions.explanation: "It seems plausible that" — hedging phrase expressing cautious possibility.

questions.question: The evidence ___ to point to a systemic issue rather than individual failings.
questions.explanation: All three work: "tends to point" (pattern), "appears/seems to point" (observation).

questions.question: It ___ reasonable to conclude that the training programme had a measurable positive effect.
questions.explanation: All three are correct formal hedging constructions before "reasonable to conclude".

questions.question: This strategy ___ likely to alienate a significant portion of the existing customer base. (is / seems / appears)
questions.explanation: "Is likely to alienate" = probably will. Direct but hedged future prediction.

questions.question: Workers in the gig economy ___ to face greater financial instability and fewer legal protections.
questions.explanation: All three are correct: "tend to face" (observed pattern across the group).

questions.question: It ___ that the current approach may not be sustainable in the long term. (would seem / tends / looks)
questions.explanation: "It would seem that" — the most tentative and formal hedging opener.

questions.question: There ___ to be growing consensus among experts that remote work is here to stay.
questions.explanation: "There appears to be" and "there seems to be" are both correct for hedged observations.

title: Concession & Contrast — despite, whereas, even though, albeit

questions.question: ___ the economic uncertainty, the company maintained its commitment to expanding its workforce.
questions.explanation: "Despite" + noun phrase. Cannot use "despite" before a clause with a verb.

questions.question: She accepted the offer ___ the salary was considerably lower than she had been expecting.
questions.explanation: "Even though" + clause (with subject + verb). Shows a stronger contrast than "although".

questions.question: The northern offices are modern and well-equipped, ___ the southern branches are significantly underfunded. (whereas / despite / even though)
questions.explanation: "Whereas" contrasts two different situations simultaneously.

questions.question: ___ being offered a generous severance package, she chose to pursue the unfair dismissal claim.
questions.explanation: "Despite" + gerund (being offered). No subject after "despite".

questions.question: He agreed to the terms, ___ reluctantly, after pressure from the board.
questions.explanation: "Albeit" = although. Used before adjectives or adverbs in formal writing: albeit reluctantly.

questions.question: ___ the training was comprehensive, many employees still felt underprepared for the new system. (Despite / Even though / Whereas)
questions.explanation: "Even though" + clause with subject and verb: Even though the training was comprehensive.

questions.question: Senior staff tend to resist change ___ junior employees are generally more adaptable.
questions.explanation: "Whereas" for simultaneous contrast between two different groups.

questions.question: ___ her lack of confidence in formal settings, she delivered an impressive presentation.
questions.explanation: "Despite" + noun phrase (her lack of confidence). No verb follows directly.

questions.question: The product was a commercial success, ___ modest, in its first year of release. (albeit / whereas / despite)
questions.explanation: "Albeit modest" = although modest. Used to qualify a preceding statement formally.

questions.question: ___ the process was time-consuming, the outcome fully justified the effort involved.
questions.explanation: "Even though" + full clause. Emphasises the unexpected result despite the difficulty.

questions.question: The policy applies to all employees, ___ those in senior leadership positions. (including / whereas / albeit)
questions.explanation: "Including" introduces members that are part of the larger group — senior leaders are part of "all employees".

questions.question: She made a valuable contribution, ___ brief, during the strategy meeting.
questions.explanation: "Albeit brief" = although brief. "Albeit" qualifies the preceding positive with a minor concession.

questions.question: ___ some team members raised objections, the decision was implemented without modification.
questions.explanation: "Even though" + clause with verb (raised). Shows unexpected implementation despite objections.

questions.question: Permanent employees receive full benefits, ___ contractors receive only a basic rate of pay. (whereas / despite / even though)
questions.explanation: "Whereas" contrasts the two different employment conditions simultaneously.

questions.question: ___ of the significant investment in marketing, sales figures remained flat throughout the quarter.
questions.explanation: "In spite of" + noun phrase. "Despite" and "In spite of" are interchangeable before nouns.

questions.question: The new manager was well-intentioned, ___ often tactless in the way she delivered feedback.
questions.explanation: "Albeit often tactless" = although often tactless. Concession within a positive overall statement.

questions.question: ___ the merger promised significant synergies, the integration proved far more complex than anticipated. (Despite / Even though / Whereas)
questions.explanation: "Even though" + clause: Even though the merger promised synergies.

questions.question: Urban professionals tend to prioritise career advancement ___ those in rural areas place greater value on work-life balance.
questions.explanation: "Whereas" contrasts the priorities of two different groups.

questions.question: ___ facing considerable internal resistance, the director pushed ahead with the restructure. (Despite / Even though / Whereas)
questions.explanation: "Despite" + gerund (facing). No subject after "despite".

questions.question: The initiative was broadly successful, ___ there were isolated incidents of non-compliance.
questions.explanation: "Even though there were incidents" — clause after "even though" showing an unexpected nuance.

title: Advanced Relative Clauses — Non-defining & Reduced

questions.question: The managing director, ___ had been with the company for over two decades, announced her retirement.
questions.explanation: Non-defining relative clause with "who" for a person. Commas show it's additional, non-essential information.

questions.question: The new branch, ___ is located in the financial district, will open its doors next month.
questions.explanation: Non-defining relative clause with "which" for a thing. "That" cannot be used in non-defining clauses.

questions.question: The policy, ___ had been under review for several months, was finally approved by the board. (which / that / who)
questions.explanation: Non-defining clause for a thing: "which". "That" is not used in non-defining relative clauses.

questions.question: The candidate ___ we interviewed yesterday has an impressive track record in crisis management.
questions.explanation: Defining relative clause — "who" or "that" are both correct for people.

questions.question: The legislation, ___ critics argue is fundamentally flawed, is due to come into effect next year.
questions.explanation: Non-defining clause with "which". The clause adds extra information about the legislation.

questions.question: Employees ___ contracts were terminated received a settlement offer. (who / whose / which)
questions.explanation: "Whose" for possession: employees whose contracts = the employees' contracts.

questions.question: The report ___ by the external auditors revealed several areas of concern.
questions.explanation: Reduced relative clause: "written by the auditors" = which was written by the auditors. Past participle.

questions.question: The employees ___ in the redundancy programme were offered retraining opportunities.
questions.explanation: Reduced relative clause: "included in the programme" = who were included. Past participle.

questions.question: The conference, ___ is held annually in Geneva, brings together leaders from over 50 countries. (which / that / who)
questions.explanation: Non-defining clause for an event/thing: "which". Adds extra information with commas.

questions.question: The deadline, ___ had originally been set for March, was pushed back to June due to unforeseen delays.
questions.explanation: Non-defining clause with "which" for a thing (the deadline). Adds non-essential information.

questions.question: The company ___ she works for has recently been acquired by a multinational. (which / that / both are correct)
questions.explanation: In defining clauses, both "which" and "that" are correct for organisations/things.

questions.question: The issues ___ in the initial assessment were subsequently addressed in the revised plan.
questions.explanation: Reduced relative clause: "identified in the initial assessment" = which were identified. Past participle.

questions.question: The HR manager, ___ expertise in employment law is widely respected, will chair the investigation.
questions.explanation: Non-defining clause with "whose" for possession: whose expertise = the HR manager's expertise.

questions.question: Candidates ___ from overseas require additional documentation to verify their qualifications. (applying / applied / who apply)
questions.explanation: Reduced relative clause: "applying from overseas" = who are applying. Present participle.

questions.question: The resolution, ___ all parties had agreed to in principle, collapsed at the final stage of negotiations.
questions.explanation: Non-defining clause with "which" — the agreement was additional context, not essential identification.

questions.question: The strategy ___ by the consultant proved far more effective than the previous approach.
questions.explanation: Reduced relative clause: "recommended by the consultant" = which was recommended. Past participle.

questions.question: The new policy, ___ purpose is to improve transparency, has been welcomed by most stakeholders. (which / whose / that)
questions.explanation: Non-defining clause with "whose" for possession: whose purpose = the policy's purpose.

questions.question: Staff ___ to remote working during the pandemic may find the return to the office challenging.
questions.explanation: Reduced relative clause: "adapted to remote working" = who adapted. Past participle.

questions.question: The CEO, ___ I briefly mentioned earlier, has an extraordinary reputation in the industry. (who / whom / which)
questions.explanation: "Whom" is used when the person is the object of the clause: whom I mentioned. Formal usage.

questions.question: The skills ___ in the modern workplace are evolving rapidly as technology continues to advance.
questions.explanation: Reduced relative clause: "required in the modern workplace" = which are required. Past participle.

topic: Be used to Get used to
title: Be used to / Get used to / Used to — Key Differences

questions.question: I ___ used to commuting for two hours every day — it no longer bothers me.
questions.explanation: "Am used to" = I am accustomed to it now. It's familiar and no longer difficult.

questions.question: It took her several months, but she eventually ___ used to the pace of the new job.
questions.explanation: "Got used to" = she gradually became accustomed to it. A process of adaptation.

questions.question: He ___ work much longer hours before the company introduced the new work-life balance policy. (used to / is used to / got used to)
questions.explanation: "Used to work" = he worked long hours in the past but no longer does. A past habit now stopped.

questions.question: She ___ receiving critical feedback, but she has learned to use it constructively.
questions.explanation: "Wasn't used to" = she was not accustomed to it at that time in the past.

questions.question: Moving abroad is a big adjustment — you'll need time to ___ used to the culture.
questions.explanation: "Get used to" = the process of becoming accustomed. It implies adaptation over time.

questions.question: I ___ a much larger team, but after the restructure I now manage just three people. (used to manage / am used to managing / got used to managing)
questions.explanation: "Used to manage" = past situation that no longer exists. A past state now changed.

questions.question: She ___ presenting to large audiences — she does it every week without any nerves.
questions.explanation: "Is used to presenting" = she is fully accustomed to it now. No longer a challenge.

questions.question: I ___ working night shifts after about three months — my body clock adjusted gradually.
questions.explanation: "Got used to" = the process of adapting to night shifts was completed after three months.

questions.question: The team ___ dealing with tight deadlines — it's second nature to them now. (used to / is used to / got used to)
questions.explanation: "Is used to dealing" = they are fully accustomed to it. Current state of familiarity.

questions.question: He ___ have a much more senior role before the company downsized.
questions.explanation: "Used to have" = a past situation that no longer exists. His previous senior role.

questions.question: It will take time, but you'll ___ used to the different management style eventually. (be / get / used)
questions.explanation: "Get used to" = the future process of adapting. You will adapt over time.

questions.question: She ___ the informal communication style in this organisation — she came from a very formal background.
questions.explanation: "Isn't used to" = she is not yet accustomed to it. Current lack of familiarity.

questions.question: When he first joined, he wasn't ___ being micromanaged — he had always worked autonomously before.
questions.explanation: "Wasn't used to being micromanaged" = he was not accustomed to it at that past time.

questions.question: She ___ live much closer to work, but she moved to a more affordable area last year. (used to / is used to / got used to)
questions.explanation: "Used to live" = a past situation that has changed. She no longer lives close to work.

questions.question: I'm ___ used to the commute now — I use the time to listen to podcasts and read.
questions.explanation: "I'm getting used to" = I am in the process of adapting. Not fully accustomed yet.

questions.question: She ___ working independently, so collaborating in a large team was initially quite challenging for her.
questions.explanation: "Was used to working independently" = at that time in the past, she was accustomed to it.

questions.question: He ___ much more responsibility in his previous role — this position feels quite limited to him. (used to have / is used to having / got used to having)
questions.explanation: "Used to have" = past state that no longer exists. He had more responsibility before.

questions.question: After a few weeks, most people ___ used to the new performance review system.
questions.explanation: "Got used to" = they adapted to the system after a few weeks. Past completed process.

questions.question: Working from home suits her — she ___ working independently and managing her own schedule. (is used to / used to / got used to)
questions.explanation: "Is used to working independently" = she is currently accustomed to this way of working.

questions.question: Moving to a country where people queue for everything takes adjustment — you have to ___ it.
questions.explanation: "Get used to it" = the process of adapting. Advice for future adaptation.

title: Prepositional Phrases — in terms of, with regard to, on behalf of

questions.question: ___ your recent application, we are pleased to invite you to an interview.
questions.explanation: "With regard to" = concerning, about. Used formally to introduce the topic of a letter or email.

questions.question: I am writing ___ the entire management team to express our sincere gratitude.
questions.explanation: "On behalf of" = representing, in the name of. Acting as a representative of a group.

questions.question: ___ cost, the second option is considerably more attractive. (In terms of / With regard to / On behalf of)
questions.explanation: "In terms of cost" = when considering cost as a factor. Used to specify which aspect.

questions.question: The new system is superior ___ efficiency and ease of use.
questions.explanation: "In terms of efficiency" = when judged by the criterion of efficiency.

questions.question: Please find the signed contract attached, ___ your request in yesterday's meeting.
questions.explanation: "As per your request" = according to, in line with. Formal business English.

questions.question: The lawyer signed the documents ___ her client, who was unable to attend in person. (on behalf of / in terms of / with regard to)
questions.explanation: "On behalf of her client" = as a representative of, acting for.

questions.question: ___ the recent changes to data protection law, all staff must complete the updated compliance training.
questions.explanation: "In light of" = considering, given. Used to introduce a reason or context for action.

questions.question: The proposal was rejected ___ its significant cost implications.
questions.explanation: "Owing to" = because of. Formal alternative to "because of" or "due to".

questions.question: She was promoted ___ her exceptional performance over the past two years. (on account of / in terms of / on behalf of)
questions.explanation: "On account of" = because of, as a result of. Formal reason phrase.

questions.question: ___ the financial risks, the board voted to proceed with the acquisition.
questions.explanation: "In spite of the financial risks" = despite the risks. Formal concession.

questions.question: ___ productivity, the home-working arrangement has proven highly effective. (In terms of / With regard to / Both are correct)
questions.explanation: Both "In terms of productivity" and "With regard to productivity" are correct formal expressions.

questions.question: The complaint was resolved ___ the customer's full satisfaction.
questions.explanation: "To the customer's full satisfaction" = resulting in, achieving complete satisfaction.

questions.question: ___ the matter you raised at the last meeting, we have now conducted a full internal review.
questions.explanation: "With regard to the matter" = concerning, in relation to. Formal business opener.

questions.question: The contract was terminated ___ the supplier's repeated failure to meet agreed deadlines. (due to / in terms of / on behalf of)
questions.explanation: "Due to" = because of. Formal causal phrase used in professional and legal contexts.

questions.question: The initiative achieved remarkable results ___ employee engagement and retention.
questions.explanation: "In terms of employee engagement" = when measured by, considering as criteria.

questions.question: She accepted the role ___ a considerable reduction in her working hours.
questions.explanation: "Despite a considerable reduction" = in spite of. She accepted even though hours were cut.

questions.question: The decision was made ___ the best interests of all stakeholders. (in / with / on)
questions.explanation: "In the best interests of" = for the benefit of. Fixed prepositional phrase.

questions.question: ___ to the above, please ensure all documentation is submitted before the deadline.
questions.explanation: "In addition to the above" = furthermore, also. Adds information to what was previously stated.

questions.question: The project was completed ___ schedule and within the agreed budget. (ahead of / in terms of / on behalf of)
questions.explanation: "Ahead of schedule" = before the planned deadline. Common business expression.

questions.question: She spoke ___ the entire workforce when she raised the issue of unsafe working conditions.
questions.explanation: "On behalf of the entire workforce" = as a representative of all the workers.

title: Noun Clauses — I believe that, It is clear that, the fact that

questions.question: It is widely acknowledged ___ diversity in the workplace leads to better decision-making.
questions.explanation: "It is acknowledged that" — "that" introduces a noun clause as the subject of an impersonal structure.

questions.question: The fact ___ she was passed over for promotion despite her excellent performance is deeply concerning.
questions.explanation: "The fact that" — "that" introduces a noun clause explaining what the fact is.

questions.question: I firmly believe ___ a supportive work environment is essential for long-term productivity. (what / that / which)
questions.explanation: "Believe that" — "that" introduces the content of the belief as a noun clause.

questions.question: It remains unclear ___ the restructure will affect frontline staff.
questions.explanation: "How" or "what" can both introduce noun clauses after "unclear". "It remains unclear how/what..."

questions.question: ___ she managed to resolve the dispute so quickly was impressive.
questions.explanation: "How" (the way she managed) and "What" are both possible as subjects of the sentence.

questions.question: The report highlights ___ significant improvements in retention have been achieved since the policy change. (what / that / how)
questions.explanation: "Highlights that" — "that" introduces the content of what the report shows.

questions.question: It is essential ___ all staff are aware of the whistleblowing procedure.
questions.explanation: "It is essential that" — subjunctive noun clause introduced by "that".

questions.question: Nobody knows ___ the investigation will conclude or how long it will take.
questions.explanation: "When" or "what" can both introduce indirect question noun clauses after "know".

questions.question: ___ he was able to maintain performance standards despite the difficult circumstances is remarkable. (That / What / How)
questions.explanation: "That he was able to" — "that" introduces a noun clause as the subject of the sentence.

questions.question: The issue is ___ the current system lacks any mechanism for employee feedback.
questions.explanation: "The issue is that" — "that" introduces the noun clause explaining what the issue is.

questions.question: It is not clear ___ the new regulations will apply to existing contracts or only future ones. (that / whether / what)
questions.explanation: "Whether" introduces a noun clause expressing uncertainty between two possibilities.

questions.question: The fact ___ the company failed to disclose this information will likely have legal consequences.
questions.explanation: "The fact that" introduces the specific content of the fact.

questions.question: Nobody could have predicted ___ the small startup would become one of the most influential companies in the sector.
questions.explanation: "Predicted that" — "that" introduces the content of the prediction as a noun clause.

questions.question: It is regrettable ___ more was not done to address the issue at an earlier stage. (that / what / how)
