questions.explanation: "It is regrettable that" — "that" introduces the noun clause after the impersonal structure.

questions.question: ___ really matters in any negotiation is the ability to find common ground and compromise.
questions.explanation: "What really matters" — "what" introduces a noun clause as the subject. Equivalent to "the thing that".

questions.question: She acknowledged ___ the decision had been handled poorly and committed to a review.
questions.explanation: "Acknowledged that" — "that" introduces the content of her acknowledgement.

questions.question: It is worth noting ___ the data from the first quarter may not reflect the full picture. (that / what / how)
questions.explanation: "It is worth noting that" — common formal phrase introducing important additional information.

questions.question: The question of ___ the company should expand internationally remains a subject of considerable debate.
questions.explanation: "Whether" introduces a yes/no question as a noun clause: whether to expand or not.

questions.question: I understand ___ you may have reservations, but I urge you to consider the long-term benefits. (that / what / how)
questions.explanation: "I understand that you may have reservations" — "that" introduces the content of understanding.

questions.question: ___ she said in the meeting suggested she was open to renegotiating the terms of the agreement.
questions.explanation: "What she said" — "what" introduces a noun clause as the subject. Means "the things that she said".

title: Future Forms — will, going to, present continuous, future perfect compared

questions.question: Look at that sky — it ___ rain heavily this afternoon.
questions.explanation: "Is going to rain" — prediction based on visible evidence (the dark sky).

questions.question: I promise I ___ contact you with an update as soon as the decision has been made.
questions.explanation: "Will contact" — a promise or commitment made at the moment of speaking.

questions.question: This time next week, she ___ her performance review with the senior management team. (has / will have / will be having)
questions.explanation: "Will be having" — future continuous for an action in progress at a specific future time.

questions.question: We ___ the project team tomorrow at 10am — it's already in everyone's calendar.
questions.explanation: "Are meeting" — present continuous for a confirmed future arrangement already organised.

questions.question: By the end of the financial year, the company ___ its growth targets for the third consecutive year.
questions.explanation: "Will have met" — future perfect for completion before a future deadline.

questions.question: I think remote working ___ become the norm for most professional roles within the next decade. (will / is going to / is)
questions.explanation: "Will become" — opinion-based prediction about the future without present evidence.

questions.question: She ___ her maternity leave in March — her return date has already been confirmed by HR.
questions.explanation: "Is starting" — present continuous for a confirmed and arranged future event.

questions.question: At this time next month, the new CEO ___ the company for three full months.
questions.explanation: "Will have led" — future perfect for duration completed by a future time point.

questions.question: Based on current trends, demand for AI professionals ___ to continue growing exponentially. (is going / is likely / will likely)
questions.explanation: "Is likely to continue" — hedged future prediction using "is likely to".

questions.question: Don't call her at 3pm — she ___ her weekly one-to-one with her manager then.
questions.explanation: "Will be having" — future continuous for an action in progress at a specific future time.

questions.question: The merger ___ finalised by the time the annual report is published. (will be / will have been / is going to be)
questions.explanation: "Will have been finalised" — future perfect passive for completion before a future reference point.

questions.question: She has already decided — she ___ apply for the position regardless of what anyone else thinks.
questions.explanation: "Is going to apply" — prior decision already made, intention formed before speaking.

questions.question: I ___ see what I can do about rescheduling the appointment for you.
questions.explanation: "Will see" — spontaneous offer or willingness expressed at the moment of speaking.

questions.question: By the time she retires, she ___ the organisation for over thirty years. (will serve / will have served / will be serving)
questions.explanation: "Will have served" — future perfect for a duration that will be completed by a future point.

questions.question: He ___ the Tokyo office next Tuesday — flights and accommodation are all booked.
questions.explanation: "Is visiting" — present continuous for a confirmed, arranged future event.

questions.question: Watch out — if you keep pushing him like that, he ___ resign and we'll lose a vital team member.
questions.explanation: "Will resign" — first conditional result: a real future consequence of current actions.

questions.question: This time tomorrow, I ___ my job interview at the technology firm downtown. (have / will have / will be having)
questions.explanation: "Will be having" — future continuous for action in progress at a specific future time.

questions.question: I think the industry ___ evolve significantly over the next five years as automation becomes more prevalent.
questions.explanation: "Will evolve" — opinion-based future prediction, not based on present evidence.

questions.question: By the end of this course, you ___ all the grammar structures needed for the B2 examination. (master / will master / will have mastered)
questions.explanation: "Will have mastered" — future perfect for completion before the end of the course.

questions.question: She told me yesterday that she ___ apply for the international posting — she had already discussed it with her partner.
questions.explanation: "Was going to apply" — reported speech of "going to" plan: prior intention reported in the past.

title: Advanced Phrasal Verbs — account for, draw up, phase out, take on

questions.question: Can you ___ for the discrepancy between the two sets of figures in the quarterly report?
questions.explanation: "Account for" = explain, give a reason for something. Used formally in professional contexts.

questions.question: The legal team will ___ a comprehensive contract covering all the agreed terms and conditions.
questions.explanation: "Draw up" = prepare, write formally. Used for contracts, plans, and legal documents.

questions.question: The company plans to ___ diesel vehicles from its fleet by 2028 and replace them with electric ones. (phase out / draw up / take on)
questions.explanation: "Phase out" = gradually eliminate or discontinue something over a period of time.

questions.question: She decided not to ___ any new clients until she had resolved the backlog of existing work.
questions.explanation: "Take on" = accept, agree to do, or employ. Take on new clients / take on extra responsibility.

questions.question: The new branch ___ for nearly 40% of the company's total revenue in its first year of operation.
questions.explanation: "Accounted for 40%" = represented, constituted that proportion of the total.

questions.question: The HR department will ___ a new set of guidelines following the review of working practices. (draw up / phase out / take on)
questions.explanation: "Draw up guidelines" = write, prepare formally. Used for official documents and policies.

questions.question: The government announced plans to ___ coal subsidies over the next ten years.
questions.explanation: "Phase out coal subsidies" = gradually eliminate support for coal over time.

questions.question: She has ___ considerable additional responsibilities since her colleague went on extended sick leave.
questions.explanation: "Taken on responsibilities" = accepted and started doing additional work or duties.

questions.question: The audit team could not ___ the missing funds, which triggered a formal investigation. (account for / draw up / phase out)
questions.explanation: "Account for the missing funds" = explain or justify where the money went.

questions.question: Before signing anything, both parties should ___ a clear memorandum of understanding.
questions.explanation: "Draw up a memorandum" = formally prepare and write the document.

questions.question: The ageing infrastructure will be ___ as part of the modernisation programme over the next decade. (phased out / drawn up / taken on)
questions.explanation: "Phased out" = gradually replaced or eliminated as part of a planned process.

questions.question: The startup ___ three senior developers last month to accelerate the product launch.
questions.explanation: "Took on three developers" = employed, hired them to join the team.

questions.question: Rent and utilities ___ for more than half of the average household's monthly expenditure in this city.
questions.explanation: "Account for more than half" = represent, constitute that proportion of total spending.

questions.question: The solicitor will ___ a settlement agreement for both parties to review and sign. (draw up / phase out / take on)
questions.explanation: "Draw up a settlement agreement" = formally prepare the legal document.

questions.question: We are ___ the use of single-use plastics across all our operations by the end of the year.
questions.explanation: "Phasing out single-use plastics" = gradually eliminating them as part of a sustainability plan.

questions.question: She was reluctant to ___ the project lead role, fearing the workload would affect her work-life balance.
questions.explanation: "Take on the project lead role" = accept and begin doing it.

questions.question: The finance director was asked to ___ for the budget overrun at the board meeting. (account / draw up / phase out)
questions.explanation: "Account for the budget overrun" = explain, justify why it happened.

questions.question: The two companies ___ a joint venture agreement that will run for an initial period of five years.
questions.explanation: "Drew up a joint venture agreement" = formally prepared and wrote the document.

questions.question: The technology will be gradually ___ as newer, more efficient systems become available. (phased out / drawn up / taken on)
questions.explanation: "Phased out" = gradually discontinued and replaced over time.

questions.question: Online sales ___ for 78% of the company's total revenue during the pandemic.
questions.explanation: "Accounted for 78%" = represented, constituted that percentage of total revenue.
